“The general thinking is that without a team spirit a group cannot win. And so, coaches essentially make a team play to win, to win his game. But naturally, every player has his own individual idea, and most of the time a champion’s game sense doesn’t agree with the coach’s idea. So at the end of the game, if they win, the coach rejoices, but the champion, he groans…because he lost the most important game, his own game.”
Is he trying to incite me against Coach Barry?

“That’s how I became MVP. It was obvious we had no chances of winning that game, but I was determined to win my own game and that was exactly what I did, and fortunately it gave the team the lead, and the coach his victory,” he grins widely obviously relishing the reminiscence of that feat. “Coach Barry told me what happened between you and the head coach what’s his name.”
“Coach Sebastian.”

“Sometimes it doesn’t matter if the team wins or not as long as you win your own game,” he says in a low tone. “It’s the same reason some good players never make the team, because they are not willing to play the coach’s game. Sometimes it becomes necessary to make sacrifices to see a dream come true, what do you think?”
I stare impatiently ahead of me knowing what he’s driving at. Bastard! You’ll never have her if that’s what you want.

Julia told me Dele asked her out the next day after the mock match, but she declined. He swore that she’d never make the team despite her impressive performance. I informed Coach Barry about it, although I asked him not to make any fuss about it, it’s possible he discussed it with him. Coach Barry told me there had been several similar complaints by the girls that Dreck told them point-blank that if they’re not willing to rise up to his indecent proposal, they could as well forget about NUGA.
Clenching my fist angrily, I glare at him as he walks away with his right hand dangling in the sleeve of his flannel shirt.
NINE

It’s nearly half past eight and I’m gussied up for the matriculation in my new black Italian suit Ifeanyi bought for me, which I reserved for the occasion. I’m not excited about the event anymore. My enthusiasm about becoming an undergraduate has been burnt off as I reflected on the import of Daddy’s weighty letter – he has formally disinherited me for rejecting God’s will for me, and he put that in writing. He’s bitter about the way I secretly left home to chase the wind, the way he put it. I will never succeed in my chosen career, he said firmly. He ended the letter with a stern warning that I should never return to his house, and that God will judge me and my accomplice for rebelling against His will.

The letter was sent through Nkeonyere when she returned to school at the end of the holiday. It’s my father’s reply to the letter I wrote to my parents informing them that after considering my father’s position about my original ambition and the trouble it had caused the family, I changed my mind about reading medicine (with the intention of pacifying them, especially my father). I also informed them about the matriculation and how delighted I’d be if they could come.
The knock on the door jerks me up from my thoughtss. The door’s handle squeaks but won’t open because I locked it. She bangs on the door furiously and turns the handle again. “Iyke, Iyke,” she calls.
“I’m coming Julie I’m almost through.”
“You better hurry up we’re almost late.”
I tear the letter and throw the shreds into the refuse basket. Then I unlock the door to let Julia in.
“Why did you lock the door?”
“Nothing,” I reply whimpering.
“What’s the matter?”
“Nothing.”

She picks up the envelope from which I took the letter, which is lying on the bed. The envelope is addressed to me, care of Nkeonyere Obiajulu. Julia’s head drops to a piece of the shredded paper on the floor. She stares at me obviously probing for words, but my lips are tightened. She drops the envelope on the bed, shrugs her shoulder without any intention to interfere and leaves the room.

Julia and I have practically become bedmates since Ifeanyi travelled. Her insomnia worsened, she also claimed to be hearing the hallucinatory voice and seeing apparition of her dead sister. But lying beside me gave her some respite. The burden of the nightmare was laid on me – while she slept I kept vigil. Her spirit also torments me at night. Initially, I kept it from Julia, but when it began to tell on me – when she began to notice my fatigue at our morning drill – I not only told her of the nightmares, but I damned the consequence and also confessed to her what really happened to Florence.

Just as I had estimated, she wasn’t upset by my confession, because the grief of Florence’s death has been assuaged. Instead, our mutual attraction for one another, which began to develop soon after she started sleeping in my room, has deepened. Because this feeling cannot be expressed in spoken words, we play the hanky-panky through the night.

Three days ago I would have had it off with Julia for the first time, were it not for the pranks Rebecca and Anita put up at their dissatisfaction of my illicit affair with Julia. After school we usually spent most of the time in the guest room behind closed doors. As a way of expressing their discontentment, the two girls have been up to the mischief of knocking at the door one after the other to say one unimportant thing or another until they succeed in getting either or both of us upset.

That day, there was a terrible vibration that came from the television speakers abruptly interrupting our romantic mood. We rushed out of the room to find there was nobody in the sitting room. Rebecca wasn’t back from school yet, but Anita was alone in the garden. Although she denied wearing the telly, neither of us believed her. Anita got angry when Julia called her a liar and shot back at Julia calling her a prostitute.

Apart from my parents, Rebecca and Anita have also declared their unwillingness to come and witness my matriculation. Rebecca, the spokesperson, standing by the car as we are about leaving the house, used the opportunity to air their disgust about our abominable affair. Julia says I should ignore them. But Rebecca’s words prick my conscience and my rumination of Ifeanyi’s warning, ‘Boy, you better be careful with Julia’, gives me deep concerns about our incestuous affair.
“I think we should stop this…this –”
She caresses my hand gently. “Is it because of what the kids said?”
“It’s not because of what the kids said, Julia. This is not right,” I hesitate, “I think you should go back to your room.”
“I can’t sleep alone,” she replies.
“You can Julia, and please don’t tell me about Florence, she has nothing to do with this and you know it.”

She whimpers about my calling her a liar. I apologize. Then we debate on our way to campus – whether it is right for one to date his or her cousin or not. When Julia sees that I’m not in agreement with her perspective of what is right or wrong, she demands that we change the topic. But it seems as if there’s nothing else for us to talk about, neither of us utters a word again.

The parking bay of the campus stadium is in chaos, volunteer Boy Scouts doing the work of traffic wardens have run out of ideas on how to control the throng of cars streaming into the small space reserved for visitors. The teenagers clad in green shirt and brown khaki shorts briskly walk to and fro in the maze. Julia tells me the procession will commence immediately after the Chancellor’s speech and suggests I run along while she looks for a space to park the car.

The ceremony is well underway, the loud speakers blare out the voice of the compere as he introduces the Chancellor to the rostrum. The crowd in the stadium roars in applause. I wade through the teeming crowd in search of the stand for the faculty of biological sciences starting with the faculty of arts, hoping biological sciences will be next, and then I discover that the faculty stands are not allotted in alphabetical order.

I feel so out of place in the stadium as I walk past family members posing for photographs with their wards and fellow students clad in blue matriculation gowns grinning from ear to ear. I’ve not had a photograph, but I’m not particular about that at the moment. I’ll have lots of pictures with Julia and Nkeonyere – they’re the only members of my family around to witness my matriculation.

I’ll make my parents regret this, especially my father, one day he’ll beg to stand by my side for a picture, when I become a famous medical doctor, when I get my breakthrough in the research for the cure of sickle cell anaemia, the same way I was celebrated at my valedictory ceremony from the secondary school, when everybody begged to have a photograph with me – the first among equals, I ponder as I walk past my colleagues who are over the moon that at last they made it into the ivory tower. I especially miss Ifeanyi today. He promised me he’d be around for my graduation from the medical school. He egged me on to repeat the feat in secondary school. He has a saying that underdogs sniff at success, but only the dogged succeed.

Luckily, I bump into one of my classmates, Charles Ikoro who directs me to my faculty stand and briefs me on the proceedings. None of these noisy chaps appear to be interested in the Chancellor’s speech. They chatter under the canopy anxiously waiting to be called by the emcee to file out for the procession, and thereafter disperse to feast with their families. Although we are a bunch from different departments, most of us attend lectures together because we take similar elective courses, and so we fraternize as if we’ve known one another for ages.

Sitting quietly at a corner I try to tune my ear to the Chancellor’s speech, but I can barely hear what he’s saying because of the noisy nattering of my colleagues. Some naïve guys privileged to own a camera have been taking advantage of the euphoria to get into talking terms with the ladies. Wearing a wide grin on their faces, they pose beside the Beauties for snap shots. Many of the photographs will end up in their albums, where they’ll be referred to as, ‘my babe’.

Maureen Oduyemi declines invitation from another ecstatic loafer for a photograph. Is she shy to take pictures or just being toffee-nosed? Perhaps she’s as unenthusiastic as I am. She’s reading combined biological sciences. According to the grapevine, she’s not hooked up, she’s probably taking her time to choose a suitable date. And many of us consider her to be the most attractive girl on campus. I’ve resisted the urge to spark off an affair with her ever since I first set my eyes on her.

She’s sitting nearly fifty metres from me and I’ve been cutting glances at her. I can still make out the light smile on her face when she meets me shaking my head in pity of the disappointed pleasure-seeker walking down the stairs after she turned him down. I smile back at her and she turns away shyly. Will she deny me if I ask her to have a photograph with me? I’m not the most sociable person though I’m easily attracted to people. Girls make me blush when they tell me how handsome I am.

Julia is escorted by one of the guests that attended Ifeanyi’s send-forth party. He came with Dickson to the party. He’s also wearing the blue gown over a pinstriped black suit, a light blue flannel shirt and well-polished black shoes made of crocodile skin.
“Did you have trouble getting a space?” I ask Julia ignoring the guy beside her.
“Not at all, the Scouts eventually got their acts together. I almost got lost looking for your stand until I bumped into Bitrus.” Julia replies.
“No, I bumped into her,” Bitrus chips in attempting to be humorous.
“Anyhow, he obliged me. You remember him don’t you, at the nightclub?” Julia asks me.
Of course I do. Bitrus and I were the youngest men at the party that night. He desperately wanted to dance with Julia at the club, but she didn’t let him.
“Hi, Bitrus Shak, petrochemical engineering,” he says with an exaggerated grin extending his right hand.
“Ikenna Obiajulu, biochemistry…how do you do?” I reply shaking hands with him.
“My friends call me Bitty,” he adds.
“She calls me Iyke.”
Julia claps her hands and says with exhilaration, “Well what do I say…happy matriculation to both of you.”
“How about a photograph,” Bitrus suggests bringing out an electronic Canon camera from a black leather holder.

Bitrus looks to me like one of the stooges peddling for pictures with any good-looking matriculating female student that comes around. God knows how many girls he’s snapped with today. Anyway, we pose in front of a passer-by for a shot. He has the nerve to ask me to snap a second shot – of him and Julia. He hurriedly wears his hood and a very wide grin and put his hand around her waist. Standing at five feet eight inches, he is dwarfed by Julia’s six feet-two-inches height. This son of a bitch is not hiding his interest in Julia. I flush with jealousy and feel like smashing the camera.
As soon as I finish taking the photograph, he takes the camera from me. “So, what’s your plan after the ceremony?” he asks generally while putting the camera back into the holder.

My plan after this boring episode is to go back home to the little meal of spaghetti and chicken gravy. That’s what Julia could afford from our lean purse. She invited a few of her friends over to the house to celebrate with me. Then we’d lock ourselves in the room and drivel afterwards…and…I’ve been fighting this thought since this Shark guy showed up. I think I’m going to finally give in to Julia’s desire.

She’s standing still and her gaze is fixed in a direction frowning. Following her eyes, I sight Nkeonyere coming to us. Both of them have not been in talking terms since she returned to school and discovered I’ve been living in Dike’s house with his three daughters. I’ve also been avoiding Nkeonyere because of her constant rebuke for accepting to stay there. Three days ago, we quarrelled over her insinuation that Julia and I are having an affair and I angrily walked out on her.
We exchange pleasantries. She greets Julia coldly, but she ignores her and turns to Bitrus instead. “So what’s your program after this dreary ceremony?” Julia asks him wearing a make-believe smile.

Bitrus is aflame. “Well uh – I’m having a small party at the palace, it’s not a crowd though just a few friends, and the royal family,” he replies blatantly making an impression about being blue-blooded.
“The palace!” Julia exclaims. I’m surprised – she’s not a material girl.
“Of course I’m a Shak,” Bitrus replies with an egoistic smile.
“So are you inviting us?” Julia responds.
“I’d be delighted to have you as my special guest. I could send a chauffeur to pick you up if you don’t mind.”
Nkeonyere’s face is etched with a mischievous smile as she watches the drama taking place between Julia and her supposed newfound love. She’s faking and Nkeonyere knows it, but I’m green with envy.

We are interrupted by the pandemonium and excitement in my faculty stand as the blaring loudspeakers announce it’s the turn of the faculty of biological sciences to come forward for the long-awaited procession.

Two men dressed in black suits with a bold tag written protocol direct us to file into a single long line. I’m trying to make up my mind whether to join them or not. I’m too jealous to leave this Shak guy with Julia. I can do without the legendry handshake with the Chancellor.
Bitrus can’t contain the joy of my having to leave him with Julia for a while. If Nkeonyere decides to wait for me, which is likely I’m not sure of meeting Julia when I return.
“Listen to me Mr. Shak or whatever your name is,” I explode, “Thanks for inviting us to the palace, but we won’t be able to honour your invitation. We’re equally expecting some guests at home.”
“You guys stay together?” Bitrus asks.
Julia and I remain silent.
“I.K. they’re leaving you behind,” Nkeonyere reminds as the long line to the podium begins to move.
“How about later, say in the evening?” Bitrus insists.
“Why don’t you just leave her alone okay, get lost!” I snarl at him.
Bitrus smiles sheepishly at Julia. “I’ll see you later okay,” he mutters and glances at me as he walks away.
Julia and I consciously avoid each other’s eyes. I hurriedly join the procession at the rear.

My mood is certainly out-of-place with that of my noisy faculty mates marching with gusto back to our stand after the handshake with the Chancellor. Some are already breaking away from the queue to continue endlessly with the photographs. I sight Maureen a few metres away ambling down to the stand with a course mate with whom she’s been chatting all morning. I presume she’s watching me out of the corner of her eye and purposely slowing down for me to catch up with her. What do I do? Request that I have a photograph with her? No that’ll be too childish.
“Hi Maureen,” I greet when I catch up with her.
“Hi,” she replies with the most charming voice.
“The name is Iyke.”
“I know,” she replies with a short smile. Her friend walks faster leaving us.
“So, uh – happy matriculation.”
“Same here.”
“So, what are you up to after now? Are you having a party also, like everybody else?”
“If I was, would you come if I invited you?”
“Dare me.”
“She won’t let you,” she giggles.
“Who?” I ask pretending not to know whom she’s referring to.
She turns her face looking ahead nodding, “She.” Julia is standing alone under the canopy a short distance away.
“She is my cousin…so what are you doing after here?”
“Since you’re so eager to know, unfortunately I’m not having a party,” she chuckles.
“Can I come and see you in your room sometime?”
“If you feel so strongly about it…she’s so beautiful. I can imagine you feel terrible she’s your cousin, don’t you?”
Maureen is a bloody mind reader.
“I’ll see you later.” Maureen hastens her steps as we approach the stand.
Nkeonyere is no longer at the stand. Julia is tight-lipped and red in the face.
“You really put up a show embarrassing me in front of everybody,” Julia berates me in subdued voice.
“In front of a Shark you mean.”
“Will you stop this barbarism?”
“Let me tell you what is barbaric, for a guy to toast a lady in the presence of her lover. Is that not undignified enough for a prince?” I shoot back.

“Lover?” Julia retorts with a tone of surprise, “You refer to yourself as my lover, Iyke,” she laughs derisively and then continues in a subdued tone, “Whatever happened to your lecture on morals.” She shakes her head. “You obviously have a dramatic way of protecting what you love don’t you?” she hesitates, “Your sister called me a harlot.”
Flushed with anger, I turn sharply to her.
“She accused me of luring you to bed.” She shakes her head in disbelief and laments, “Am I seducing you?”
I can’t think of an answer to that question.

“I’m sorry I did,” she says closing her eyes and taking a deep breath, “I’m disappointed that you had to wait until someone else expresses his feelings for me before you could get the nerve to do the same?”

My knees buckle. I take the nearest vacant seat and ponder over my intimacy with Julia. If she’s keen to hook up with this Shark guy, I won’t stop her. When I turn to Maureen’s direction, I meet her stare. She winks her right eye at me and turns away. Does she have a hint about what has just transpired? The matriculation goes on without me.

My days in my uncle’s house are numbered. Julia has been keeping all of us miserable wearing a very long face and nagging all the time over insignificant issues. She has clamped down on all recreation activities – banned all of us playing music in the house, it’s nuisance she says. She forbids playing scrabble, chess or any other games her sisters usually play when they return from school. She thinks it’s now a distraction and orders them to face their studies.

They inwardly mock at her squabbles in the heat of the moment, and when she’s away, they amuse each other by taking off her rant and rave over trivialities. I suspect their real pleasure is the fact that our indecent romance has finally gone sour.

In order to make my stay worthwhile, I have taken to tutoring Rebecca and Anita after school. Rebecca will be sitting for senior school certificate examination in May. She said her father wants her to study Law. Anita says her ambition is to become a medical doctor. When I put them through an aptitude test, I discovered that Rebecca has tremendous aptitude for science subjects, particularly physics and mathematics. I encouraged her to read computer science instead. Anita was disappointed with the result of the test. Her aptitude for biology is unimpressive for her career choice, but she’s too self-willed to be dissuaded. She’s quick-witted and has confidence in her ability to learn, she also possesses an enviable dedication for whatever interests her. Because I don’t want to quench that fire, I’m putting in extra effort to develop her knowledge in the subject.

Julia and I no longer go to school together. She drives to school with her sisters while I take the bus. A deep-seated resentment has replaced our once romantic affair, there’s no love lost anymore between us, and she takes delight in embarrassing me at the slightest opportunity. I avoid her hostility as much as I can although I can’t explain why I’m gradually beginning to enjoy her frustration at my indifference to her malice and loneliness.

Sani peeps at me from the square hole in the gate when I press the bell. The bars squeak and the pedestrian gate opens. I moodily wave in response to his greeting as I ease into the house. A blue Nissan Bluebird is wrongly parked in the compound nearly blocking the front walk. I can hear the sound of music playing aloud in the sitting room and when I come closer, Julia’s loud laughter.
It’s Rebecca who answers the doorbell. She mumbles a greeting looking sad. I reply casually. She returns to join Anita at the dining table where they’re studying. Anita stutters a greeting.
Julia is sitting in the long sofa with the Shak guy I met at the stadium, “Welcome Iyke how was your day?” Julia greets obviously trying to conceal our malice.
“I’m fine and how was your outing?” I reply politely hiding my jealousy. There’s no need washing our dirty linen outside after all.

“Beautiful. Bitty has been spoiling me with a trip to some nice places in town,” she says with the intention of poking out my envy. But she won’t get it because I’ve been with Maureen, even though I told the girls I’d be going to the basketball court to train. Rebecca is apparently annoyed by her sister’s affectation and sighs.
“Oh where did I leave my manners,” she giggles and suddenly turns to Bitrus, “Bitty you remember Iyke, don’t you, my cousin?”
“Of course I do,” he stands up and we shake hands. “And I almost forgot to tell you what an extraordinary athlete you are…at this rate you could be the man to watch at the NUGA.”

“Thanks.” I peek at Julia out of the corner of my eye. Her pretence has faded away and her face is now blank. Something tells me Julia doesn’t fancy this guy and I’m afraid she’s insinuating herself into this relationship as a way of getting back at me.
“Have you heard of a guy named Dreck?” Bitrus Shak asks.
“You mean Dele Tanko?”
“The legend of the campus basketball team,” he enthuses.
“So what about him?” I ask.
“I just want to know if you’ve had a chat with him,” he says. “I think you’re going to break his record of twenty eight points at the NUGA.”
“That’s an awesome achievement. That’ll be a difficult task.”
“His game is nothing compared with yours Iyke. I played B-ball in secondary school too,” he informs me.
“I give it to Julia she’s helped me recover most of my skills, but I know I still got a lot of work to do to improve my form.”
She smiles.
“So did you take part in the team selection?” I continue.
“My liver failed me when I considered contesting with giants like you…well uh – I actually got discouraged when after school I discovered I couldn’t grow any taller,” he says jokingly.
Rebecca and Anita chuckle, Julia too finds his joke amusing.
“On a serious note I’d like us to discuss the basketball team later,” Bitrus adds.
“No wahala…excuse me. I need to freshen up.” I hit the room for a shower.

Bitrus is joining us for dinner and he’s occupying my seat at the table. Julia is sitting next to him at the end of the table, while Rebecca and Anita are seated in their usual positions. My plate is set in Florence’s former position and food is served in each person’s plates, a deviation from the norm. Dinner today is fried rice garnished with green beans, carrot and cabbage, and barbecued chicken.
I take my seat quietly. “Shall we say the grace,” I ask.
“The food has been blessed,” Julia replies flatly.
Julia is looking for an opportunity to pour out her grievances, but I won’t let her have it, not in front of her visitor. Her eyes drop on the table and she uncovers her plate.
“That’s not true,” Rebecca shoots back at Julia.
“I said the food has been blessed, are you deaf?” Julia snarls at her.
“When?” Rebecca insists.
I glance at Bitrus Shak. He’s apparently feeling uncomfortable with the argument going on between the two sisters.
“It’s okay Becky,” I break in to forestall any quarrel, “It’s not always necessary for us to pray aloud.”
Rebecca makes the sign of the crucifix and starts eating. Cutleries clink against our plates in the hush. Then Anita stands up carrying her plate to leave the table.
“Where do you think you’re going, Anita?” Julia asks her.
“To – to – to my room,” Anita stammers.
“Will you sit down and eat your food,” Julia orders her.
“She has a right to eat wherever she wants.” Rebecca blasts at her.
“Will you shut up? Julia shouts at Rebecca. “Get back to the table Anita.”
Anita drops her plate on the table, sits and folds her hands refusing to eat. Rebecca sighs, carries her plate and leaves the dining to the sitting room daring Julia.
It seems that Bitrus has lost his appetite. He whispers to Julia, then gives me a short smile and pushes his seat backwards, “I’m afraid I’ll uh – be on my way. Goodnight Iyke.”
“Goodnight Mr. Shak,” I reply.

Julia sees him off to the car. I can hear the Bluebird raving. Rebecca comes back to the table sighing. Anita sneaks out of her room and returns to her food. Julia locks the front door. She’s taken aback by the unanimous clinking of plates as she turns towards the dining. The girls ignore her. I pity her.

“I think it’s high time you moved into the hostel,” Julia says after taking her seat at the table. “To avoid any more trouble,” she continues, “Besides, I don’t know how Daddy will feel if he returns to hear the way Ifeanyi left us without his knowledge, plus I don’t know how he’d take the death of Florence.”
Didn’t I see the writing on the wall? She’s so unforgiving. I wish I didn’t tell her how Florence actually died. I brace myself.
“What’s eating you up Julia? Where do you expect him to go now?” Rebecca attacks her.
“Oh come on shut your trap will you?” Julia replies.
“It’s okay Becky,” I say calmly.
“No it’s not,” she jolts up. “Don’t shift the blame on him Julia. If anybody is responsible for the death of Florence, you should be the one.”
She gives her a smile that says, well I don’t blame you or, I wish you knew what really happened to our sister.
“How could you be so mean …you selfish bitch,” Rebecca blasts at her.
Julia stands up angrily and gives her a hot slap, “Don’t you ever talk to me like that. Are you out of your mind?”

Rebecca sighs and leaves the table angrily. Anita’s eyes are bloodshot, but she remains silent. I doubt if she’ll be able to utter a sentence at this rate. I am struck with a feeling of guilt for the death of Florence, and regret for telling her the truth.
I should have listened to Ifeanyi. She’ll certainly tell them how Florence died, that I’m responsible for her death. I think it’s better for me to just leave this house.
TEN

Apart from the hoop at the garage where I slogged through the drill in preparation for the university tournament, which I miss, I consider my leaving Uncle Dike’s house an advantage to my bid for a place in the school team – it has given me the much-needed focus and frame of mind. Although, on the downside, I have to travel some twelve kilometres to get to where I practise, with the locals, but generally speaking, my game has improved tremendously within a few days.

My drill now includes sprints and workout every morning. In the evening, I jog my way to the grotesque basketball pitch where I engage the local basketball players who know very little about the rules of the game, and even if they do, they consider it an obstruction to our very physical kind of game. Perhaps the most salient objective in our court is to get the ball past the opponent by all means and pass it through the ring, which as one would expect, has neither a net nor a backboard.

It was when I started playing with the group that I introduced the office of an umpire, a secondary school student, who looks forward to coming to watch me play, but he is always reminded at the start of each match by the locals not to interrupt the game unnecessarily or they’ll take the whistle from him. Hence our game looks more like a rugby match than a basketball contest. What a way to train!

A round trip to my new training ground, a rundown secondary school basketball court, costs approximately eighty Naira. In order to conserve my lean purse, I jog to the school, and after the bullfight I take the bus back home.

Early in the morning before going to school I skip and work out in my personal gym to strengthen the muscles. My gym comprises basically of dumbbells and barbells I fabricated by cement cast from various sizes of used paint cans and different sizes of metal weights mainly of parts of vehicles that I gathered from a mechanic. I feel like I’m in the best of shape.
When I told my teammates about the friendly match my coach arranged against the Plateau state team, they were excited and unanimously agreed to come and see the game and cheer me on.

Coach Barry is exploiting his position as coach and ex-player of the all-season favourite in the national league to arrange the friendly match as part of our warm-up games ahead of the NUGA competition. And he told us frankly that we would be playing against their star-studded championship squad.

Many of the old players in the school team have been weeded out of Coach Barry’s squad, but a few relics that were called up for the friendly match at the insistence of Dele also kitted in the yellow jersey and trunk are perpetually kept on the reserve bench. I gather from an older teammate that the former coach, Louis Usman was sacked after a lacklustre performance in the last NUGA competition. Coach Barry is not taking any chances.

Presently, the match is not going in our favour, but the margin is not as wide as most of us feared. We are giving the champions a good fight for it. The female team have come to spur us on to victory. I doubt if they’re expecting us to win. Nevertheless, their presence here may be the real reason we’re rising up to the occasion. Three of my teammates – Dotun Mobolaji, Ephraim Haastrup and Abdul Jima – are dating members of the female team and they’re outplaying themselves in order to impress the girls, who are looking stunning in their yellow jersey. My cousins, Julia, Rebecca and Anita are also in the gym. Julia is not wearing the school’s jersey, however. Dele made good his threat.

Coach Barry is the one who usually calls for timeout, since he doubles as the coach of the state team, and I doubt if his assistant bothered to come for the match (after all we’re rookies), the champions merely drivel with one another at break up until the umpire signals for the game to commence. He charges me, Dotun and Ephraim – recruits from the mock matches – to play as if we’re in a tournament, and he’s banking on us.

At every timeout we reassure ourselves of the possibility of turning the game around and probably win it, and Coach Barry bolsters our confidence. At every timeout I steal glances at Julia, but she consciously avoids meeting my eyes – that’s usually when she prefers to be pointing out one thing or the other about the game to her sisters. I’ll talk things over with her as soon as the game is over. I also steal a glance at the giant digital clock in the hall that tells us how many minutes remaining in the game. I’m marking my time so as to play solo as soon as we enter the last ten minutes of the match.
We are not likely to win the friendly against the champions, but I’m definitely set to win my own game.

It’s ten minutes to the end of the game, barring timeouts, it’s time for Rhizopus to be reborn, time to display my celebrated moves in court. The score line at timeout is seventy-two to fifty-eight. I flag off the change of tempo with an incredible three-point shot from the centre circle less than a minute after the umpire blew the whistle ushering us in. The margin is narrowed to eleven. They dribble to our side, but unfortunately miss their scoring chance. Ephraim wins the ball. He sights me and makes a long pass for a counter attack. I beat their last man and take a wicked dunk that sends shocks up the spines of the champions, and boosts the morale of my teammates.

Less than four minutes of play, the champions are still in the lead, but the score line is eighty-five to eighty-two. Naturally, there is anticipation that three pointers will be appropriate at this precise moment, but that’s not my plan. I switch position with Dotun who had been playing defence and retreat to play point guard, the passes are deft and accurate and the crowd here is thrilled. I keep our opponents zipping about and after us for about a minute. Then I bolt past the defence like a whirlwind with a stunning crossover and score.
Dele is the first to shout, “Go Rhizopus! Go! Kick their ass!”

And for the first time the champions are forced to call for timeout. Unlike the previous times when they chat among themselves and even slight us by signing autographs for their fans, they’re forced to knock their heads together or lose the game to these rookies.

Rebecca and Anita blow me a kiss and I return it. Julia is no longer with them, but I know she’s somewhere in the court, she won’t leave the kids. My local teammates give me the two-finger victory sign. Dele winks at me and gives me a thumb up. The crowd shrieks after him clapping their hands, “Rhizopus! Rhizopus!!”
They’re expecting the magic, and I’m the Genie. Rhizopus is reborn.
As we walk into the court for the last minute of play, Coach Barry beckons on me, “Don’t do it,” he says.
“I hear you Coach. I won’t,” I reply.

The Rockets have a record of winning the league five times and are dreaded in the national basketball league. If we win them here, it’ll be an affront to the coach, not the players that a mere college side beat the state champions. I will give Coach Barry his due respect. And no one must hear our little gossip, Coach Barry and me.
Although our team will lose the friendly match, I have won my game. And I’ve also won the hearts of the fans here.
ELEVEN
“I call that superlative. No one has ever played like this after Dreck,” Dele says smugly.
“Thanks Dreck.”
“You won the game,” he says.
“I took your advice,” I reply.
He looks to his left then to his right and whispers, “Your team, I mean, but you lost. You shouldn’t have listened to him Iyke.”
“What do you mean?”
“It was quite obvious you intentionally let them win. He asked you to do it, didn’t he?”
“I don’t know what you’re talking about.”

“Anyway don’t bother.” He gives me a short smile and turns to the left, then to the right again to see if anyone is coming. “Since you have proven to be good at keeping secrets, and since you are already a champion,” he says with slight hesitation, “it’s no use keeping this away from you. Have you heard of the Golgotha?”

Golgotha is the place where Jesus was crucified, but that’s not the one he’s asking about. I got to know from Dotun, my teammate that there’s a clandestine cult that goes by the name Golgotha and that the basketball team has been their stronghold since Dele’s outstanding performance at the NUGA. They rule the basketball court, and they’re sacrosanct about having all the players initiated into the brotherhood. Legend has it that previous coaches had either been members of the widely spread cult group or at least sympathised with the cult’s sly activities in the court.

“I know you have a dream Iyke. But it’s up to you now to decide whether you want that dream realised or whether you want it to die,” he says as he brings out something from his pocket and makes sure it’s concealed in his left hand, while squeezing it into my palm.

It’s a small card shaped like a coffin. It’s getting dark, but I can see faintly the diagram of skull and crossbones on the card. The symbol on the card gets adrenalin flowing. I’m not interested in belonging to the Golgotha or any cult group, and I make him know exactly that.

Dele laughs it off and tells me, “It’s a necessary evil if you’re dreaming of making the team list to the NUGA competition. We’re playing tonight ten p.m., basketball court. It’s strictly for champions. For your information, all the stars who play here are Golgites.” He taps me on my shoulder and says, “See you at ten.” Then he leaves.
There’s another inscription in the inner page of the card, The Place of the Skull 00: 01.

I know exactly what he means by saying that the game is strictly for champions. Seventy players were initially short listed after the first round of the mock games out of which fifty players are pencilled as eligible members of the school team. Most of them performed poorly at the trial games. But Dele included the names of players that are members of the Golgotha fraternity.
Coach Barry adopted a strategy to weed them out at the final screening that will take place in a fortnight, and further reduce the number of eligible players to thirty.

Initially when Coach Barry named us, and we heard about the Golgotha, we all swore under our breath not to have anything to do with the cult, but we were told by older teammates that when the chips are down many of us will go on bended knees to belong. In spite of the mix-up Dele stirred in the selection of players that will make up the team, instinctively, he’s aware of the few good hands among the newly recruited players that are likely to scale through the screening, those are the ones he refers to as champions.

Regrettably, when I inform Coach Barry of what Dele is doing on the sly and even show him the card, it dawns on me he is aware that this is going on, but obviously winks at it. He asks me if the others have paired up, and gives me an ambiguous ‘think about it Iyke’ response in a subdued tone of voice. Indirectly telling me he doesn’t have the clout to challenge the influence of the Golgotha over the players, and because his primary concern is to win the trophy, I guess he doesn’t mind doing so with a squad made up of skeletons and ghosts. I tell him point blank that I’d rather forfeit the NUGA than join the Golgotha fraternity.
I unconsciously caress the pendant of the gold chain on my neck. Raising the pendant slowly, I contemplate the image of the crucifix.

On the eve of Ifeanyi’s trip to the States he made me swear by the Almighty God, in whom he doesn’t believe, that I’ll never join the cult, and gave me this gold necklace with a cross pendant as a token of that vow. I’ll uphold that oath no matter what it’ll cost me.

Although my faith in God has waned so much since my coming to Jos, because I no longer have the urge to read the Scriptures or pray as I used to, the pendant reminds me of my vow and resolution to shun the cult. Not even the threats of this megalomaniac, who thinks he and his bunch of mischief-makers run the school, will make me change my mind.
I learn from Dotun’s comments about the fraternity that I’m the only player among those recently recruited that didn’t attend the initiation. I tell him sternly, I won’t join the bandwagon.

Sitting at a deep corner in the tiny newspaper section of the library in the second floor of the building, I’ve been trying to do what Coach Barry advised me to do – thinking about it. About what precisely, I can’t tell. I’m also staring at the book that has been lying open in front of me for the past one hour or so. I don’t know exactly which is making me woozy, but my mind is in a fog.

I’m also listening unconsciously to distinguish the familiar sharp knocks of her shoe heels from the clumsy shuffle of visitors to this quiet area of the library, whose clogs seem to be too heavy to be lifted off the floor.

I know I’m risking losing Maureen by taking her to my crib today, but I can’t evade her request anymore. She’s been asking me to take her to see where I live, but because of the condition of my room, I’ve been evading her request.

After my sudden eviction from Uncle Dike’s house, I moved into this bizarre one-bedroom apartment. It’s so deplorable, it’s only fit for one desperately in need of shelter, but, it’s home all the same, in the meantime at least. It’s virtually the only place I could afford at that time.
I catch a whiff of a familiar scent. The intensity increases with the sound of heavy footsteps coming towards me.
“Hello Iyke.”
I look up from the reading desk. It’s Bitrus Shak.
“Hi Bitrus.” I remember he hinted wanting to discuss something about the team.
“May I sit?” He asks pointing at the chair. I placed one of my notebooks on the table to my left to reserve the seat for Maureen.
“Yeah sure,” I respond taking my notebook away.
“I regret the trouble my visit caused you,” he says as he drops his notebook on the table. He draws the chair and sits down. “Julia told me you no longer stay with them.”
“Well, yeah. I moved out,” I reply droopily.
“So how have you guys been coping?” Bitty asks smiling.
“If you don’t mind, I’m not in the mood to talk about Julia okay.”
“Women,” Bitty giggles, “I hope you’re not also fooled by her sour grapes.” He fiddles with a bunch of keys and eventually drops it on the table. “She’s madly in love with you, Iyke.”
“Julia is my cousin,” I growl back at him.
“I’m afraid she doesn’t see you that way. You and her need to talk.”
“Are you here to speak on her behalf? Is that why you’re here?”
“Yes and no.”
“I squint at him.
“In principle yes…but on a serious note, I think she wants to see you.”
“Looks like you know a lot about women?” I say smiling.
“I know something about Julia,” he pauses. “she loves you.”
“Have you ever been in love with your cousin, Bitty?” I ask in a subdued tone.
“If I have cousins as beautiful as Julia I wouldn’t mind dating them all,” Bitty replies smiling.
“You’re crazy.”
“Who cares?”

“She nearly starved me to death, systematically depleted my purse by making me take the bus to and from school, and eventually threw me out of the house without a dime and without minding where I’d be putting up…does that fit your description of a woman who is in love with a guy?”
“You’re the one who’s mishandling the whole issue. That’s what I noticed at the dining table that day. She was using me to get back to you. But you were too jealous to see it.”
I ponder over what Bitty says for a while. “She doesn’t respond to me when I greet her.”
“That’s actually why I wanted to see you. She’s hoping you can get Dreck to put her back in the team,” Bitty pauses waiting for me to respond.
“I see. Well, I’d advise her to forget about the NUGA games and face her studies.”
He doesn’t say anything.
“What do you know about Dreck?”

“Dreck,” he repeats awe-inspiringly, “The basketball icon…after watching your friendly match with the state team, I said to myself a new legend is about to be revealed that will definitely surpass the spectacular final match of the nineteen eighty-four NUGA competition in the University of Ibadan. That’s where Dreck was made.”
“You think I’m better than him?”

“Are you kidding me? You made Dreck look like a choirboy. I saw the look on his face. Damn! Was he not stunned?” Bitty takes a pause shaking his head. “You guys have some things in common. A good game sense and he’s such a utility player too,” his attention turns to two girls entering the library. He turns and waves at them.

One of them dressed in flannel check shirt and a black skirt waves back in our direction the second time. I could swear this one is for me but Bitty grinning widely returns her greeting. She stares at me for a moment, and then looks away with a fallen face as they take their seats at a table backing us.
“What else do you know about Dreck?”

“He also made his outstanding debut as a first year student. But unlike you, he never was a big man. He had a rare opportunity to show his skill when he was brought in as a substitute in the fourth quarter of the final match less than five minutes to the end and the team was ten points behind. He brought the team back and secured their victory with a spectacular three pointer from the centre circle in the nick of time. The game ended in a slim one point difference in their favour…Dreck became a hero,” Bitty concludes with a reverent smile etched on his face. “Can I ask you something Iyke?”
“Yeah sure,” I reply.
“Did you play the game with a talisman?”
I stare at Bitty with a curious squint.
“I’m sorry it’s kind of uh personal.”
“It’s not personal Bitty. It’s absurd,” I reply raising my voice.
“It’s on the grapevine,” he says “Somebody is definitely spreading the rumour.”
We stare at each other silently for a moment.

It is common practice for teams to employ the services of a necromancer to secure victory at competitions. My mind goes back to this age long practice in my secondary school days. I’ve never participated in it. I remember most of our players originally declined but later changed their minds because of the suspicion that their opponents would use such negative spell against them, and the fear of being outwitted by opponents who were by all estimation less skilled made them give in to using talisman.
“The same thing was said about Dreck. His teammates accused him of using charms in the game.”
“Was it true?”

“How would I know? He didn’t bother about his critics anyway. Many were of the view the rumour was the handiwork of teammates who envied his feat and fame. But many people believed it because he never really played a fantastic game after that tournament.”
“What do you think?”

“Dreck thrilled his fans so much they didn’t bother to know what inspires him.” Bitty sniggers, “The end justifies the means you know. Sport is a funny thing. As long as you’re winning, you have the applause of your fans, but they’ll call for your head if you let them down.”
“The end justifies the means right?”

“Yeah,” Bitty replies, “Why do you think athletes still fail dope tests in spite of persistent warnings by sports regulatory bodies? Has any athlete ever been abandoned by his or her fans because he or she failed a dope test? The end justifies the means, that’s the rule of the game.”
“I guess you missed a vital line in that script…if you’re not caught in the act.”
He remains silent.
“Well, going by your premise, I suppose you advised her to do all it takes to make the team, did you?”
“Hey man,” he hushes me looking around in shame, “What do you take me for?”
“I see, so your theory depends on the person involved? Well if they sent you to come and talk me into joining the Golgotha, better go look for somebody else.”
“Hey,” Bitty replies angrily, “I don’t have anything to do with the Golgotha okay. I’m only here to –”
“Speak for her,” I snap, “I know.”
“She’s sorry about the way she treated you, believe me.”
I respond with a chuckle and check the time. It’s three forty-five and Maureen is not yet here. Ignoring Bitty, I turn to my book pretending to be reading.
“Dreck is a bitter man, Iyke,” he continues, “If Julia doesn’t yield to his wish, she won’t participate in the competition.”

“Listen Bitty…I…I’m not taking this personal with Julia okay. She’s my cousin for Christ’s sake,” I frown. I don’t know Bitty too well to bare my mind to him, but I guess he won’t stop pestering me if I don’t give him a concrete reason for not rising to his request. “Dreck has forced me to take a difficult decision myself. You may be the first person to hear that I won’t be going to the NUGA,” I breathe through clenched tooth.
“What! What do you mean by that?”
That’s exactly the way Coach Barry responded when I told him I’d rather quit the game than join the Golgotha fraternity, although he believes I’ll change my mind when I give thought to it.
“You thought I was one of them, didn’t you?”
“You’re not a fan of the team?” he asks.
Whatever that means. “Now you know I’m not a member of the Golgotha fraternity, and I don’t play the game with talisman,” I add with disdain.
“I’m sorry.” His eyes dart about the room again as if he’s suspecting somebody to be eavesdropping on us.
One of the girls who waved to us has left her seat and she’s walking towards the door.
“Excuse me,” Bitty whispers and stands up following her.

I turn back to my book, to see if I can concentrate. But rather I find myself trying to digest Bitty’s food for thought. Who does Dreck think he is anyway? Suddenly, there’s a loud sound from the dropping of a pack of books on the table.

He is sucking a lollipop noisily wearing a pair of sunglasses, a newly grown dark goatee on his chin and there’s an unfriendly note in his smile. He is dressed in a dirty stonewashed jeans and a black tee shirt. He rudely pushes Bitty’s books aside and sits down.
“What the hell do you think you’re doing?” I bark at him.

He slowly takes the sunglasses away and charily hangs it on his breast pocket and replies, “What does it look like?” His eyes are bloodshot and bleary, and from the veins sticking out of the side of his head I make out he’s definitely stoned.
“Can’t you see the seat is occupied?”
“Mind your language. I wouldn’t be sitting on a chair if there was somebody on it, would I?”
We stare at each other for a while. He smiles and turns his face away.
“I have a message from Dreck,” he whispers, “He wants to know why you didn’t come for the game?”
“Tell him I said he can go to the devil and play his stupid game okay.”
“I hope you know who you’re referring to?” he giggles, “You can’t be in the team if you…”
“Who the hell do you think you are?” I snap with clenched lips, “Listen, I’m not interested in your stupid game. Now will you let me concentrate on my studies for Christ’s sake!”
“Hey, what’s the matter Iyke?” Bitty asks me as he comes back to the table.
I do not respond.
He turns to him and asks, “What’s the matter, Femi?”
“Ask your friend,” he replies smiling mischievously at me.
Bitty silently packs his books from the table and walks away.
“Bitty! Come back,” I order him. Then I turn to the crazy guy. “I told you the seat is occupied now will you get your dirty ass off the chair?”
“It’s okay Iyke. I’ll fix myself up somewhere else,” Bitty bleats feebly.
“No you’re sitting here,” I insist.
Femi is pretending to be reading and sucking lollipop noisily.
“Get up and get lost asshole.” I tap him on his shoulder
“Iyke please leave him alone,” Bitty pleads.
“No. You will sit here. I told him the seat is occupied.”
“Iyke listen to me,” Bitty begs me.
“I said get up are you deaf?” I tell him.
“So what if I refuse?” he finally replies.

Bitty stands in between us stopping me. I push Bitty aside grab his shirt and raise him up from the chair. Then I kick the chair away from under him and let him fall freely. He hit the ground on his ass. Suddenly there’s a rumbling of seats and people rushing into the room from the nearby section.

He picks up the sweet from the floor put it in his mouth and stands up. He wears his sunglasses and looks around the room. Those who had come from the adjacent section to watch a fight stream out of the room, while those reading in the newspaper section return to their seats disappointedly. He chuckles and turns to me.
“What’s the problem Femo?” another boy wearing a blue jeans and a navy blue blazer asks him.
“This idiot is picking a fight with me,” he replies smothering his anger with a smile.
“Is it true Iyke?” he asks me.
Two library attendants come in to see about the altercation. “What is all the noise about, what is happening here?” one of them asks.
“There’s no problem sir,” Bitty replies smiling, “Everything is all right now.”
Femi picks up his notebooks from the table and leaves the room accompanied by his friend.

The library attendants glare at Bitty and me and leave the room. The boy sitting next to my table begins to clap his hands, several seats shift noisily, and the clapping of hands intensifies. Then they all stand in ovation. Bitty and I smile at each other.
TWELVE

He is so well informed about campus cult groups that I wonder what tickles his interest about their deplorable clandestine activities. Bitty’s commentary on campus fraternities is also giving me the impression he’s nursing the interest of joining the cult. His down-to-earth nature is completely out of place for a blue blood. I’m beginning to think it’s an expression of his low self-esteem.
“No one is allowed to join the team if he hasn’t signed up with the Golgotha fraternity,” Bitty says as he drives me back to my apartment in his car. “Do you know why Dreck insists on that?”
“He used to be a member of the group called the Tornadoes. But he broke away from the group with some of its members after a problem about sharing the loot from a robbery operation…”
“They go for robbery?” I break in.
“So I heard,” Bitty replies, “Dreck and this other guys left the Tornadoes to form another cult group.”
“Golgotha.”

“Exactly,” Bitty affirms smiling, “The original members of the Golgotha were Dreck’s teammates. Dreck had a personal interest in gathering the talents to himself and forced new players to join his group, that’s how their membership grew until they outnumbered the Tornadoes and after several fights, they forced them out of the pitch. In retaliation, the Tornadoes attacked him and shot him in his right hand to make sure he never plays the game again... permanently. Dreck swore that anybody who would play in the school team must become a fan of the basketball team.”
“How did you know this?”
“The cult is not as secret as they make it look, Iyke.”
“I guess so. Well, thanks for the ride.” We shake hands and off I go.
“Hey! What’s your plan for Valentine? You got a date?”
“Yeah.”
“Maureen? She’s not in town. Didn’t she tell you she’s married? She’s gone to be with her hubby.”
I’m dumbfounded.
“A friend invited me to a party, if you don’t mind I can come and pick you up tomorrow.”
“Yeah sure. Goodnight.”
“Goodnight.”

If Bitty’s right about Maureen being married I’ll make her pay for leading me on. But what can I do about it? Beat her up. No… Rape her! No. I could be sued, and even go to jail if I try to hurt her in anyway. Maybe I’ll just forget about her.

When I met Bitty up campus, in the afternoon, I informed him about a change in our planned trip to the venue of the party because I needed to see Nkeonyere, my sister in her hostel. I suggested that we meet at the party venue, but he told me he doesn’t know where it’s taking place. Valentine, the celebrant promised to come and take him to the place.

He explained that he deliberately concealed information about the venue to keep gatecrashers from having a prior knowledge of the place in order to curb their nuisance. Bitty and I eventually agreed that he’ll wait for me in his apartment at eight o’clock.

I can hear the sound of music playing in the room, but no one has answered the door. If Patricia, her roommate is the only one in the room, then she won’t answer even if I knock on the door for eternity. She’s hard of hearing. I knock on the door again and wait for response.
Nkeonyere comes from the room opposite room 107, “Hello I.K. how are you?”
“Good evening Nkay.”
“Have you been knocking for long?”
“No. How is Bisi?”
“Oh, she’s fine you want to say hi to her?”
“Maybe later.”
“Please come in.” She opens the door.

Patricia is in all right. She greets me in her usual misnomer, Andrew. Wherever she got that name, it doesn’t sound anything like Ikenna. I got tired of correcting her. I now go by the name Andrew in room 107. She’s a diploma student of social works, but spends most of her time drawing, it’s her favourite pastime.

She happily shows me a half-completed portrait of Nkeonyere she’s doing. Nkeonyere says she’s been at this since morning. Compared to the image in the photograph she’s copying, the portrait has the slightest resemblance of her, but I’m sure by the time she’s through Nkeonyere would pay her anything, to have it destroyed. I give her a nod in commendation of her effort. She smiles and continues.
When I break the news to Nkeonyere that I no longer live in Uncle Dike’s house, her reaction is not far from what I expected, but she’s not pleased that I’m staying off the school premises.
“It’s better for you to put up in a manger than spend one night in his mansion,” she says, “But what about the hostel accommodation assigned to you?”
“That’s old story, Nkay.”
“What do you mean that’s old story?”
“Somebody else took the space.”
“You should have reported to the student affairs to get the person evicted, the room is allocated to you, how could you have allowed that?”
“I don’t have the strength to start struggling for a bed space with some crazy guys. Besides, my new apartment is very comfortable.”
“It serves you right. I wonder how Ifeanyi could convince you to stay in Uncle Dike’s house with that harlot.”

“Stop that Nkay please!” I snap, “How could you say such a thing about her?” I glance at Patricia. She stares at me as if she’s listening to our conversation, “You embarrass me with the way you prejudge our relationship.”
“You’re the one making a fool of yourself Ikenna.”
“I’m not as unreasonable as you think Nkay. Uncle Dike is not around and the girls need help. Can’t you see that?”
“Do you know what people think about both of you?”
Nkeonyere hauls me over the coals when I emphasized with the f-word that I care less about her prejudice.
“Where did you pick that foul language I.K?” she asks frowning. “Have you lost your manners?”
“I’m sorry,” I reply flushing.
“Don’t you ever use that foul term before me again,” she warns me angrily.
“I’m sorry.”
Nkeonyere heaves and shifts deeper into her bed resting her back on the wall. “What was it you wanted to see me about?”
“It’s about Ifeanyi. I had a terrifying dream about him last night,” I pause, “I think he may be in some trouble.”
“Tell me about the dream.”

“I saw Ifeanyi chained in his hands and feet and laid in a coffin, a black coffin. Then I saw another man that had Daddy’s face come to the coffin. He shut it and orders four hefty men wearing black masks to take it away. While they were carrying the coffin, I noticed a sign of a crucifix on the cover but the cross was upside down. They took the coffin to the edge of a cliff down a pit, the pit was very deep.” I gaze at Nkeonyere.
Her eyes squint as she listens to me.

“Suddenly, a strong wind began to blow resisting them as they came close to the edge of the pit. Then a man full of white beards and dressed in white cloth appeared from the wind and rebuked them, they left the coffin and ran away. Then he spoke a word and the coffin opened,” I hesitate, take a deep breath and turn my face away from her probing eyes. “When the coffin was opened, I …I saw myself in the coffin. Then I woke up from sleep…I’ve been troubled since then Nkay. I’m afraid something bad has happened to Ifeanyi.”

Nkeonyere is gifted in the art of interpreting dreams. She’s the only one I could trust to decipher the spiritual angle of the dream and tell the meaning. Her gaze drops to her laps. After a while she looks up to me, “The dream is not about Ifeanyi. It’s about you I.K.”
I’m short of words.
“But I’m trying to figure out the connection with Ifeanyi,” she continues, “Tell me what happened since you guys came to Jos.”
“It’s a long story Nkay.”
“Forget about the details just give me the gist.”

She insists on hearing about my affairs with Ifeanyi, so I give Nkeonyere an expurgated account of what happened from the death of Florence to how Dickson helped me gain admission into the university. I also told her about Ifeanyi’s relationship with Uncle Dike, relating to the land he sold to him, and the promise he made to take me to America when he is settled.
“Apart from managing the pharmacy for Uncle Dike, what else does he do?”
“Well, I suspect he does drugs, but that’s nothing to take seriously. I don’t have a proof about that, do you understand?”
Nkeonyere looks askance at me obviously hoping to hear more.

“He told me…he’s been looking for an opportunity to leave the house. He was more or less enslaved to Uncle Dike. He said he had to leave the house before he returns or else Uncle Dike wouldn’t let him go.”
“Since when did you start taking everything Ifeanyi says for it?”
“I knew he wasn’t lying Nkay.”
“How are you sure he didn’t cook up the story just to get you to sympathise with him. Why did he persuade you to stay in his house?”
“It was my volition to stay there. I felt constrained to stay in the house to – look after the girls.”
“Was that what he was doing for Uncle Dike, looking after the girls?”
Patience is no longer drawing. I’m worried about the way she’s gazing at us. I have a feeling she’s listening to our conversation.
“Don’t bother about her. She doesn’t hear us,” Nkeonyere reassures me. “When is he coming back?”
“Sometime this month,” I reply and glance at my watch.
“Well, God will certainly reveal the meaning of your dream to us. Thank God you’re no longer in his house and do keep Julia at arms length or anything that will link you to Uncle Dike…he’s evil.”
“Is it true what happened between him and his wife?”
“Just keep your distance from him.”

Alero and Rose, Nkeonyere’s other two class-conscious and snobbish roommates bump into the room breaking in on our conversation. We exchange pleasantries and they compliment my dressing and the expensive perfume I’m wearing, one of the perfumes Ifeanyi left for me when he was leaving. They usually refer to me as Jambito, a mock term for a fresher. The ever-demanding fashion freaks tease me about a Valentine date and remind me a third time as I leave the room with Nkeonyere to bring the perfume I’m wearing when coming tomorrow.

“I wouldn’t have noticed you’re overdressed tonight if Alero hadn’t mentioned it,” she says as she sees me off. She whines about the general notion of Valentine Day celebration, and complains that it has now become a licence for debauchery and lewdness.
“A friend invited Bitty and I to his birthday party.”
“A party on Wednesday? That’s out of place,” she comments.

Only a freak would attend a party on a Wednesday night, and I’m not one, I’m too introverted for that. The only reason I accepted Bitty’s invitation is that I thought I needed a break from the worries muddling up my head by the day.
“You better be careful. You never can tell. Sometimes these cult boys lure people into their group in the name of having a party, especially if it’s taking place on a weekday.”
“No Bitty’s too lily-livered for that.”

“You can’t be too sure, I.K. I hear they’re the most susceptible candidates, especially when they feel oppressed and can’t keep a relationship, they jump at the slightest opportunity so as to muster up the clout to woo a babe, especially nowadays that some girls actually fancy dating cult boys.”
“He’s not the type Nkay, he’s a blueblood.”
“There are all sorts in the cult I.K. blueblood, purple blood, brown blood,” she replies humorously.
Although she sounds very serious, I giggle.
“I don’t feel good about you attending this party, I.K.,” she nags.
“I was waiting for you to say that. I’ve already promised Bitty.”
“Tell him I made you not to.”
I remain silent.
“Do I have your word on that?”
“Okay, okay,” I reply half-heartedly.
“I’ll pray about the dream and let you know if I receive anything.”
“Thanks Nkay. Goodnight.”
“Goodnight I.K. Take care of yourself.”
“I will.”

Bitty’s already dressed for the party. He’s wearing a black blazer, a heavy cotton shirt and a black jeans. I apologize for my lateness as I ease myself into his palatial two-bedroom apartment. He’s still waiting for Valentine to come, he says. Bitty offers me a drink. When he starts talking about Julia, my desire to be with her is awakened as I begin to reminisce those unforgettable moments, especially that romantic moment at the nightclub.
Bitty answers the knock on the door. Three hard-faced visitors dressed in dark jackets and jeans walk imposingly into the room.
“What’s up Bitty are you ready?” one of them asks him.
“Yes I am. You’ve not met my friend Iyke. Iyke, Greg,” Bitty introduces us.
I shake hands with Greg and his friends.
“I’ll constrain you a little, Greg. If you don’t mind I’d like to pick somebody at St. John close,” Bitty tells Greg.
“Where is St. John close and who the hell stays there?”
“My girlfriend and that’s in G.R.A.,” Bitty replies.
Greg and his friends laugh scornfully at Bitty. Bitty eyes me and shrugs.
“We’re already late Bitty,” Greg says looking at his watch, “Besides girls are not invited, there’ll be more than enough at the party.”
“Where’s Valentine,” I ask looking askance at Greg.
Greg gives me a look of who the hell is this guy.
“He’s busy organising things,” replies one who refused to mention his name when I introduced myself to them. He adjusts his green beret.
“We have to pick the babe,” I reply.
The boy wearing a green beret comes to Greg and whispers to him.
“All right let’s go,” Greg giggles.

Greg and I wait patiently for Bitty to lock the door. He has a mischievous smile etched on his face, which makes me suspect something ulterior is going on. Is Bitty a part of this? After locking the door, Bitty glances at me wearing a sheepish smile. I wish we could talk over this party business, Bitty and me.

We’ll be going to the venue in a ramshackle Peugeot 505 parked outside the gate. The driver, also wearing a green beret, is nodding his head to music playing in the car. Nkeonyere’s warning about attending this party comes back to me. Jesus Christ! It suddenly dawns on me this is a ploy to trick us into their cult group. I pull Bitty’s arm and stop him from entering the car.
“What’s the matter you forgot something?” Bitty asks me.
“No. Let them go Bitty,” I whisper.
“Iyke,” Bitty whispers back to me.
“What the hell is happening?” Greg blasts.
“We’re changing our mind about the party,” I reply.
The guy behind the wheels steps out of the car puffing a cigarette.
“Who the hell is this asshole Bitty?” Greg snarls at him.
“It’s all right Greg,” Bitty answers him, “Hey, Iyke, these are my friends okay.”
“Shut up Bitty!”
Greg glares at me.
“Tell Valentine or whatever he’s called we’re sorry we can’t make it to his damn party,” I yell at Greg.
“Bitty, I thought we had an arrangement, what’s all this?”
Bitty eyes me.
“Let’s go,” he whispers to the driver. “Hey nigger, I’ll make you regret this insult.”
“Go to hell!” I reply.
The rickety car puffs to life and jerks away noisily.
“What’s the meaning of this, Iyke?”
“You’re setting me up Bitty. I can’t believe you’ll think of setting me up despite expressing my feelings about Dreck and the Golgotha.”
“What the hell are you talking about?” he replies.
“Don’t pretend you know nothing about this?”
“About what, what the hell are you thinking?” Bitty yells at me.
“This is a ruse to get me to attend a cult initiation, isn’t it?”
“How can you say that, are you implying Valentine is in the cult?”
“Doesn’t it sound weird that girls are not invited to the party?”
Bitty squints at me speechless and breathing heavily.
“To crown it all the green berets they’re wearing.”
“Valentine is a cultist!” Bitty whispers, “I can’t believe this.”
The time by my watch is quarter to nine. “If you don’t mind Bitty, I have to be on my way.”
“I’ll drop you off.”

On our way home Bitty narrates to me how he and Valentine knew each other. They grew up together in the neighbourhood and attended the same schools although he’s a year ahead of Bitty in class. He is a part two political science student. “Valentine was a weakling back in our school days,” Bitty snickers as if he finds the idea of Valentine being in the cult amusing, “I’m surprised to know that he could muster the courage to join the cult.”
“Anybody can join the cult depending on why the person is sought after,” I say droopily.
“The cult is for hard men. Valentine is a sissy.”
“But he’s rich…that’s why they took him. And he is used in turn to fish for influential people like you.”

Bitty gives me a quick glance and a consenting silence. He pulls near a pathway leading to the apartment house where I live. The light beams shine on a shop a few metres away, it is closed and Richard is not on the bench, instead a girl wearing a shiny pink sleeveless dress is sitting alone on the bench in front of the shop.

The Golgotha has been breathing down my neck since I declined Dele’s invitation. I suspect they’re using Richard to monitor my movement to know when I’m around and when I’m out. Richard stays in the second room in the apartment block, two rooms away from mine. Since after my confrontation with Femi in the library, he’s made a habit of sitting on the bench at the shop, alone, puffing cigarette and sipping on a small bottle of locally distilled gin pretending to be whiling away the dark hours, but I suspect he’s monitoring my movement. I am braced for their threat.
Bitty turns the lights off and put off the ignition. “Hey, thanks…you saved me. I owe you one man.”
“You know what my Dad used to tell me, bad company corrupts good character. Be careful with those you call your friends,” I reply.
“Hey, Iyke look,” Bitty whispers pointing his finger ahead of us.
“You gave her my address?”
“She asked for it yesterday.”
“Hello Iyke,” she greets with an erotic smile that reveals the dimples on her cheeks. She’s wearing braids, and looks ravishing under the moonlight.
“Hi Julie,” I reply looking up to her from the window.
Bitty lowers the music and they exchange pleasantries. I open the door and step out of the car.
“Why did you have to come so late in the night?”
“I’ve been sitting there for nearly two hours waiting for you,” she answers pointing at the shop.
“Oh I’m sorry.”
“It’s okay. Your friend kept me company.”
“Richard?” I ask.
“Yes,” she replies. “He left about half an hour ago with one other guy that introduced himself as Femi, said I should tell you he came around.”
I grimace at Julia. Bitty jolts out of the car.
“Is anything the matter? Why are you guys looking at me like that?” Julia asks.
“How’s Rebecca and Anita,” I reply covering up our reaction.
“They’re fine and they’re definitely missing you like hell. They’re eager to see you again,” Julia replies.
“Why don’t you guys go inside? I’ll hang around so I can drop Julia later,” Bitty suggests.
“Don’t bother Bitty,” Julia replies smiling, “What if I decide to spend the night with my cousin?”
“Who wants to play gooseberry?” Bitty replies.
“There’s something I’d like us to discuss, Iyke. Can we go inside?” she says holding my right hand and making me follow her.
“I’ll see you guys tomorrow then,” Bitty greets us and shut his door. The engine raves and he honks at us. We both wave him goodbye.

What I call home is a far cry from the room I used to occupy in Uncle Dike’s house. Nearly all the asbestos ceiling boards are stained from rainwater dripping from broken roof. When I moved in I couldn’t afford a rug or carpet to cover the bare cement floor with cracks and potholes here and there. I inherited a not-so-comfortable piece of furniture that I call a chair from the last occupant of the room.

Dragging the chair a safe distance away from the severely cracked walls held together weakly by patches of cement, I bid Julia to sit down. Instead she pushes aside a mixture of clean and dirty clothes heaped on the narrow three-by-six feet mattress placed on the floor, which barely accommodates my massive physique and sits.

I offer a weak apology for the condition of the room, telling her how I’ve been preoccupied with too many things recently. Even though our nerves are on edge because of the long period of malice, I try to put up an air of nonchalance as she darts about the room in disbelief.
“I’m sorry about the way I treated you.”
“Please Julie I should be apologizing to you for what Nkeonyere said about you. I guess I was too proud to own up to it.”

“Please don’t give me that macho man stuff,” she says. Then she scowls at the wall as if she’s afraid they’re going to collapse. The walls have several cement patches covering deep cracks and painted light blue. Her eyes move to the termite-infested wooden window behind me. “Becky and Anita said I should ask you to come back home,” she whispers almost whimpering.
“Here is home Julia,” I reply with a grin, “I think it’ll be better for us to stay this way Julie.”
“Daddy called to tell us he’ll be coming back on Monday. He asked that Ifeanyi should come and pick him at the airport.”
“Did you tell him Ifeanyi travelled?”
“No…would you mind helping us out?”
“I’ve never been to Lagos.”
“I know the way.”
It would be an interesting trip after all, an opportunity for me to visit the big city. “When are we leaving?”
“Next week Thursday, I made arrangement for us to spend sometime with a family friend before his arrival so we can use the opportunity to visit some places,” Julia grins.

The rhapsodic Caribbean music playing, which I borrowed from Bitty brings back the memories of our night out at De roof nightclub, the exotic dance and the drivel in the car. I’ve never been so close to a woman, unfortunately my first love happens to be my first cousin.
“Do you care to dance?”
It’s too late for her to go back home. Is she planning to spend the night with me?
“Dance with me please,” she emotes stretching her right hand to me.

From dancing, we kiss, and then she flutters around my waist at my belt buckle. But we’re abruptly cut off from the mood by the knock at the door. Suddenly there’s a bang at the door and it flies open. I jerk up from bed. Femi and three other boys, including Richard are standing at the door. He smiles mischievously with a lollipop in his mouth.
“My, my, are we not right on time,” Femi says smacking his lips.
Julia pulls the bedspread desperately to cover her bare skin.
“What do you want?” I bark at them standing up.
“I came to keep my promise, to make you as miserable as I promised you I would,” Femi replies with a wicked smile.
“Who the hell do you think you are? How dare you bump into my room like this?”

Julia leans forward to take her dress, but one of the boys walks past me and snatches it from her. I rise to my feet and push him away angrily. He slaps me with his backhand. I land a blow on his chin in return and he falls down, I rush him on the floor to finish him off. The click of a pistol by my ear makes me freeze.
“Let him go,” Femi croaks.

I ignore him and hit the boy again. He hit me with the butt of the pistol and my head spins, I go dizzy and fall down. Julia struggles to cover herself with her arms around her chest. One of them slaps her and she screams and suddenly goes numb.
“Stay away from her I’m warning you,” I bark at them. “I swear I’ll kill you if you touch her.”
“I told you I’d get back at you, didn’t I? Nobody messes with me.”
“Please leave her out of this I beg you. Don’t do this please don’t, don’t do this I beg you,” I plead holding Femi’s legs.

“Get away from me,” he snarls at me kicking me again and presses the nozzle of his pistol hard on my head. “Consider this a Valentine Day miracle. You’re lucky to have her around. I had in mind to keep you perpetually out of the court since you refused to join us.” He signals Richard to take his pistol and keep me still and starts to unlock his belt buckle.
“Please leave her alone, I beg you in the name of God,” I sob.
“Welcome to the valley of the shadow of death, where God doesn’t exist,” Femi says as he pulls my gold necklace and cut it from my neck.
“Shoot me please…shoot me,” I whisper to Richard.
“That’ll be a waste. I’m sure after this you’ll reconsider our proposal,” Femi replies as he pulls down his trousers.
I turn my eyes away from looking at him and bow my head to the floor. “Shoot me please,” I mumble.
If I survive this, if I ever survive this, I’ll make them pay for this. I’ll surely avenge her no matter where they run to. I’ll hunt them and kill them all, I mutter under my breath.
THIRTEEN
As soon as the aircraft touches down on the runway and the overhead loudspeakers in the arrival lounge announce it’s the flight from Bangkok, Anita’s eyes light up.
“Is it Daddy’s flight?” Rebecca asks.
“Yes Becky... I think so,” I reply flabbily.
The two girls glance at each other and flush with excitement.
Rebecca turns to me and asks excitedly, “Will you come back to stay with us if Daddy asks you?”
I rub her head and look towards the herd of passengers disgorged by the plane streaming to the immigration desk for a routine check, “Let’s go.”
They follow me to the immigration desk. As soon as they sight him, they jolt with excitement. Rebecca points him out to me from the crowd streaming to the check-in point.

He’s wearing a flattop haircut and a thick moustache, and he’s dressed in a grey suit. The black attaché case in his right hand and his self-assured gait gives him the air of a tycoon who is just returning from a business trip rather than an ex-convict. From a distance he’s a spitting image of my father except that he’s fairer in complexion.

He waves to his cheering daughters as soon as he sights them. But the smile on his face soon vanishes. He looks on absent-mindedly probably dismayed about Julia’s absence. His brisk steps are slowed down as he approaches us. Rebecca and Anita race to him, while I amble on behind them.
Standing a few metres away, I watch him embrace them and chat briefly with them grinning from ear to ear and caressing their faces. I flush with loneliness and tears cover my eyes.
Uncle Dike looks up and calls, “Ikenna.”
“Welcome uncle.”
He beckons me to come to him. His eyes are deep-set and he’s slightly taller than my father. He hugs me lightly and examines me closely. “How’s the Bishop?”
“He’s fine.” Now I know how Ifeanyi came about the name Bishop.
“And how is your mum?” he asks.
“They’re both fine Uncle.”
“Where’s Ifeanyi?” he asks generally.
“He’s travelled,” Rebecca answers in a hurry itching to talk with him.
“Travelled?” he asks in bewilderment.
“To America,” Rebecca continues.
He stares gloomily at me and asks, “Where is Julia?”
I hesitate for a while. Then I respond, “She’s in Jos she couldn’t come with us.”
“That is quite obvious. Now why is she not here?”
“She’s uh –” I flounder.
“She’s not right Daddy,” Rebecca snaps.
“What’s wrong with her, Becky?” he asks.
“Let’s talk about it when you’re a bit relaxed, Uncle,” I reply and stretch my hand to take his brief case from him.
His deep-set eyes dim further. They first fall on Anita. She looks at her sister. He follows her gaze.
“How is Florence?” he trembles.
Rebecca’s eyes drop to the floor.
“She’s dead,” I announce.
He rallies quickly from the news of Florence’s death and the uncertainty about Julia and hands me his briefcase. We all head to the car park.

Rebecca is sitting at the back with her father. Although he pretends to be listening, his fiery stare tells me he’s not interested in her natters about school. He’s having the hunch that something bad has happened to Julia. When our eyes meet in the rear-view mirror, he smiles and turns to Rebecca.
“Becky, I bought you some very interesting law books,” he says.
