On each visit, Harry has a new and different message for us and at the end he invites us to commit our lives to Christ, he would also request that we confide in him and tell him our needs after which he’d pray for us and with comforting words tell us he’d continue to pray for us. I’ve never answered his call to accept the lordship of Jesus, neither have I ever asked him to pray for me.

Today, Harry is preaching a heart-searching sermon about the two thieves who were crucified with Jesus to an enthralled audience sitting in a cramped room nicknamed the Gavel. Leaning on the wall where I usually stand with hands folded, I listen with obvious disaffection as Harry renders a matter-of-fact account of the incident as if he were on Calvary hill at that precise moment. A strong feeling of guilt sweeps over me as he begins to narrate the confession of one of the thieves at the crucifixion of Jesus. At the end of his message, Steve and a few others walk up to Harry, in tears, in response to his call to come to Christ and receive the gift of salvation.

This emotional response occurs nearly every time Harry preaches, and Steve always leads the procession. But today, there’s something unique about their response to Harry’s, or the Lord’s call in his words – they’re all weeping as they march to Harry and either kneel down or sprawl on the dirty floor at Harry’s feet whimpering like little children begging God to forgive their sins. Harry prays to God to pardon their sins in the name of Jesus. Unable to bear the guilt feeling from watching this bunch of criminals, I quietly leave the room.

When I return to the Gavel after the session of prayers to receive journals from Harry, I find they’re all sitting down and beaming with smiles at the assurance that they now have a part in God’s kingdom and their names written in the book of life. Great! What an illusion?

Harry disappointingly informs me that he was unable to bring any journals for me. In his habitual good nature, he apologizes and promises to make it up on his next visit. But there’s something else I want to ask Harry.
“Can you do me a favour uh – Brother Harry?”
He smiles widely. I know, Brother! I could call him anything now – evangelist, apostle, prophet, bishop or whatever – anything that’ll make him help me deliver this letter to Uncle Dike.
“I’m sorry I can’t do it, please,” he whispers.
“Why? It’s not a difficult place to locate –”
“We’re not permitted by the prison authority to courier mails for inmates I’m sorry I can’t,” he explains.
“There’s nothing incriminating in the letter, you can read it if you like, it’s my uncle okay.”
“Please Iyke try and understand, it’s the prison regulation.”
“I’m sorry to have bothered you,” I reply and walk out on him.
“Iyke, Iyke, wait a minute.”
I walk away from him in annoyance without responding to his call.

Despite my disinterest in his message, Harry and I usually chat for a few minutes about politics, world events and anything of interest after his preaching session. He frequently asked after my family, but I usually avoid the topic. It’ll break his heart to hear that my father is a priest and that I was once a chaplain. And then he’d have a strong reason to persuade me to believe in the gospel, so I don’t bother to discuss that aspect of my life.

I have consciously nurtured this relationship with Harry because I have this gut feeling that he could help me leave this place. But if Harry is unwilling to simply help me deliver a letter, I can’t trust him to take a higher risk for me.

I was told that there’s no way out of this gaol except I’m acquitted of my charges by a Judge, but the general attitude towards engaging the services of a lawyer is doom and gloom. In my own case particularly, the oddities surrounding Sephia’s death weigh so much against me. Nevertheless, I’m still optimistic I’ll get the opportunity to get out of here somehow, someday.

But for now, I’ll have to trust that Steve’s plan will work. The plan is to negotiate with the deputy prison warder, Superintendent Afem to let me participate in the prison community service, and then while out there, he’ll help me escape. Detainees who have been sentenced and whose terms are nearly completed are occasionally deployed to designated parts of the city to do menial jobs. And apart from the community service we are totally cut off from the society. Supro Afem is in charge of selecting menials for that work.

No prisoner has ever bolted from Katampe from community service, I sold the idea to Steve. He is counting on Supro Afem being his tribesman to deliver. Though he didn’t say so, I think the deputy warder will consent to the idea if the pay is good. I sent a coded message to Uncle Dike hinting him I’ll need money to execute the plan.

I managed to get a letter across to Uncle Dike through a prison staff. I suggested the plan to him. In his reply he said he’s been in troubled waters for a while now, and presently he’s still trying to settle down with his family in Lagos. But he promises to help me and asks me to hold on until things die down. Hold on to what?

Mr. Sanusi, the pot-bellied prison warder with wicked tribal marks that slightly disfigured his nose at incision reminds us, as he does every morning at the parade, that it is because of criminals like us they have a job, and that he will do everything to protect his job. Everybody understands Mr. Sanusi is indirectly, but sternly, telling us that the severest actions will be taken to ensure that none of us escapes.
The morning parade and traditional roll call is over and eighteen of us called out are instructed to stay behind while the other inmates are ordered to go back to their wards.
“Thanks Mirage,” I whisper to Steve, “I will surely pay back for this kindness.”
“Don’t say it Iyke,” he says under his breath, “And don’t think of me okay.”
“I’ll come and get you out of here, I swear.”
“I said forget it. They’ll kill me before the case is over.”
“There are other means, trust me.”
“You’re the most daring young man I’ve ever come across,” he says eyeing me for a moment. His lips thin into a smile and he shakes his head. “Where do you get these crazy ideas?”
“One day, when we’re out of here and it’s just the two of us in a restaurant, I’ll tell you all about me.”
“Take care of yourself.”

We are quickly summoned into the waiting Black Maria one after the other. Supro Afem turns away from my stare gazing steadily into space. I hold back tears as I watch Steve turn his back without waving goodbye.
The door of the Black Maria is shut and its siren wails. We are transported to an undisclosed venue of our assignment.
EIGHTEEN

“Left right, left right…hop high…hop high…att – en – tion!” My heart sinks at the sound of soldiers on parade. Their harsh voice gradually fades out as we take a sharp right bend and the ramshackle truck cranks away.
Suddenly the van stops, the door opens and the guards usher us out. My God! We’re in a military barracks.

The duplex where we are detailed to work is littered with debris of wood and bricks from ongoing refurbishing work. Young cadets who are obviously excited to own a rifle and eager to prove their skill with the ammunition hanging on their shoulders are guarding the building. They are keeping watchful eyes on everyone of us. Escorted by three prison guards that came with us, the foreman takes us around the building and designates us to where we are required to work.
I try to make eye contact with Supro Afem, but he’s consciously avoiding me. “Son of a –”
“Why are you delaying your job?” Mr. Sanusi interrupts.
“I uh – I was about to ask for more fingers, sorry more nails,” I bleat.
“More nails?” He looks at his watch, “You have less than twenty minutes, Mr. Obiajulu.”
“But sir –”
“You have twenty minutes…and we’re not leaving anybody behind,” he snaps with a wicked smile as if he’s reading my mind.

When I was booked in, I was asked whether I possessed any vocational skills. I said yes, carpentry. I learnt it from my father – it was his hobby. Actually, he used to be a joiner before he answered the call. I undertake the repairs of furniture in the prison and sometimes get an extra meal for that, nothing more than a meal. For that reason, I’m detailed to refurbish an old wardrobe that is among the debris. I overheard the covetous warder telling the foreman he’d come and pick it up later for his personal use.

I purposely hid the nails I was given and requested for more as a devise to get the opportunity to talk to Supro Afem. He’s intentionally keeping away from me. With the presence of these chaps with rifles, an attempt to escape from here is like running through a minefield. They’ll happily waste their cartridges on me.

“By the way I thought you were supposed to be a biochemist where did you learn to make furniture?” Mr. Sanusi asks me nodding his head like a male lizard in appreciation as he walks round inspecting the wardrobe.
“My father does carpentry part time.”
“Part time?” he asks.
“Yes…he’s a preacher,” I reply.

“Really, and you are a murderer.” He gives me a thin smile. “You obviously didn’t learn anything from your father, Mr. Obiajulu. You are a wasteful carpenter in that case. Unfortunately there are no more nails,” he says and turns around to leave. “You better start thinking of a way to complete the work or else I’ll put you in the dungeon.”
“I know you’re biased sir. I don’t know who killed Sephia, believe me,” I say with deep emotions.
Mr. Sanusi halts and turns around.
“I know you’re a religious man Mr. Sanusi and a law-abiding citizen. Does the law permit a man being condemned before his trial and conviction for a crime?”

“You’re making plans to be arraigned in court, I know. But I won’t give you that privilege,” he growls glaring at me. “When you have used up the last nail, use your fingers instead, how about that Mr. Obiajulu?” he growls and returns to see what the others are doing.

The single nail I left in the packet brings back memories of the past and flashes of the series of crucifixion parades that ended the lives of the three guys that raped Julia comes to my mind. I tremble and shut my eyes, but the thought lingers.

The warder is pointing towards me as he and Supro Afem chat with two cadets. I take the hammer and pick up the nail in the packet. Placing it on the wood, I angrily whack the nail into the wood. I hit the wardrobe so hard repeatedly until the wood begins to break. The cadets rush towards me, push me down and thump my head with the butt of their rifles. They hold me down and handcuff me.
When we return to Katampe prison, the prison warder orders his men to confine me in the dungeon.

My company in this dungeon are the ghosts of Florence and Dreck. Whenever Dreck’s ghost appears, I have the horrific feeling someone is trying to strangle me. And when Florence breaks up hysterically, a hazy female figure that introduced herself as Ighmiha emerges in a dazzling white wedding gown and then she sings a melodic love song with a voice that reminds me of Sephia.
Uncle Dike is driving through a path in a forest at night escorted by a man whose face is familiar. He stops the car at a certain place and turns off the ignition.
“Where is this place and why are we here?” the man asks Uncle Dike.

“Endor…to see the Oracle. Now let’s go.” Dike steps out of the car. “You know you’ve really got us into a mess,” he says as he locks the doors, “What the hell were you thinking when you chose that kid to be Hangman?”
“Who else?” Dike yells in exasperation, “Are you crazy?”
“But you accepted him.”

“You forced him on us. What was I supposed to do? Shit! What was I thinking? Now will you shut up and let me think,” he looks around in anticipation. “Ssh,” he hushes him, “He knows we’re here,” Dike whispers.
“Who?” He asks in a subdued tone.
“The Oracle, you moron,” Dike drawls irritably.
“How come you never told me about him?”
“Because you never asked,” Dike replies with sarcasm.
“How am I supposed to ask about what I don’t know?”
“Will you shut up!”
“What do you seek Amphibious?” A deep quaky voice asks him.
Dike bows his head in the direction the voice came. Suddenly, an aged man dressed in old ragged cloths with a staff in his hand appears.
“I have come to offer oblation to Abaddon, my god…the king of the universe,” Dike replies still bowing his head.
“And you brought another infidel along?” the Oracle asks him.
“He is the cross-bearer,” Dike replies.
“Ha ha ha ha ha ha….” he laughs hysterically, “The cross-bearer indeed. Come closer let me show you what you seek.”

They draw near to where the aged man is standing. He draws a triangle on the ground with the staff he’s holding then he bends down and draws a bird, a bow and arrow, and a key inside the triangle with his index finger. “This is the future,” he sighs.
“Please tell me, what is the meaning of this?”

“These represent three disciples who will have authority in the group,” he explains pointing at the bird, the bow and arrow and the key with his staff, “In the future they shall receive a kingdom from a strange one and …”
“And what? Dike quizzes him.
Dike follows the Oracle’s blank stare and our eyes meet. I squint at him speechless. The Oracle stands up supporting his frail stature with the staff.
“What about the triangle?” Dike quizzes him, “What does it stand for?”

“He doesn’t belong to us Amphibious.” He eyes me wickedly. “But you have given him the authority and the glory of the ancient kingdom…he shall rule over you.” He drops his gaze almost immediately after talking, turns and walks away.
My uncle gazes at me with bewilderment for a moment, and then pursues the aged man.
“Go away Amphibious your son has breached the marriage vow with Ighmiha, who can appease the wrath of the sea goddess.
“Please help me Oracle,” Dike pleads holding his cloth. “I’ll give whatever oblation my god demands of me.”
The aged man turns around and gazes at him. “Follow me!”

We all wade through the dark forest following him until he stops at a small pool of water. “Wait here,” the Oracle tells Dike. He gathers some dry wood near the pool of water and blowing air at it the wood comes aflame. Then he brings a small basket made of cane, and the tusk of an elephant and motions Dike. “Come.” He gives the basket to Dike and asks him to hold it. I watch him with amazement as he pours the liquid from the tusk into the basket and the fluid is not dripping from it.
“I believe you know what to do?” He asks Dike.
“Yes I do Oracle. Thank you,” Dike replies and heads to the pool of water. One step at a time he gradually descends into the tiny pool of water until he disappears.
The Oracle squats by the fire and fans it with a fan made from palm fronds. “You may need to sit down, it’ll take some time before he returns,” he tells me.
“So you are the Oracle?” I ask as I squat in front of him.
He doesn’t respond.
“Where has he gone?” I quiz him.

“To the bottomless pit to make atonement to Abaddon, prince of the bottomless pit,” he replies slowly. “He brought upon me the guilt of an innocent man.” When he looks up at me I notice his eyes are all white. “Take,” he hands the fan to me. “Keep the fire burning, if it goes out, then he will never return…his life is in the fire.”
“How long?” I shout after him.
“Six hundred and sixty six days according to the number of a man.”

When I notice the fire going down, I fan it vigorously, but the wood smoulders and then it goes out completely. I tremble. His voice echoes in my head as I watch the aged man walk away. “Six hundred and sixty six days, six hundred and sixty six days…” his voice echoes.
It is so dark in here. I don’t know when I’m dreaming or when I’m awake, but this scene has occurred three times.

Iron bars squeak and the door opens. Two prison guards help me out of the dark room. My pupils contract from exposure to sunlight and I block the rays from my hurting eyes with my left hand. Supporting my frail figure on their shoulders, they lead me through a corridor to the bathroom.

The prison warder directs me to discard my fetid prison uniform into a cellophane bag. There is no lather from the locally-made concoction of sodium chloride and palm oil in wax intended to be soap, but I am refreshed. I receive a new pair of shorts and a shirt from the prison warder.
They guide me gently into the visitors’ room.

He stops drumming his finger and squints at me exploring my bleak gaze at him. After several months in the darkroom I discover painfully that I’m almost going blind. I can’t make out the face of the man sitting on a chair in the waiting room and if I’m not colour blind, I think he’s wearing a grey suit. The drumming of his fingers on the table tells me he’s either been waiting for quite a while or he’s naturally an impatient person.
“Excuse us,” he tells them. There’s an authoritative tone in his voice.
“You have five minutes,” one of the officers replies impolitely.
“Fifteen, I’m his attorney.”
Without further argument, the iron door squeaks.
“Sit down Mr. Obiajulu,” he motions me to a chair. “My name is Babade, Segun Babade. I’m a lawyer,” he says in a clipped sharp tone, “You may just call me Segun.”
“Ikenna Obiajulu,” I reply curtly.
“I know,” he says smugly extending his hand. We shake hands and he sits down facing me. “I believe you know why I’m here?”
I do. “Let’s not waste our time with this Mr. uh -”
“Segun.”
“I can’t afford a lawyer okay. I mean I don’t have a dime, do you understand?”
“Yes I do,” he replies chuckling. “But I’m not here to haggle about a fee with you Mr. Obiajulu.”
“What do you want from me?” I snap.
“Well, Mr. Obiajulu I hope you have been duly informed that your trial will commence on the seventeenth of next month.”
“A trial?”
“Yes, a letter was sent to you to that effect.”
I grimace surprisingly at him.
“Are you saying the letter was not delivered to you?” Segun asks opening his black leather attaché case and brings out a sheet of paper, “here.”
I take it from him but the letters are blurred so I give it back to him, “I’m sorry I can’t read it.”
“I understand.” He reads aloud the content of the letter. And then put it back into his briefcase. He takes a notepad and a pen. “How long have you been in the dark room?” he asks.
“What is today’s date?”
“Fifth.”
“Month?”
“July,” he replies.
I’ve been in the dark room for nearly three months! Segun writes on his notepad.
“So what did you do?”
“I thought you should have known that?” I reply impatiently.
“I mean, why they put you in the dark room, Mr. Obiajulu,” Segun explains, “You mind if I call you Iyke. I have a couple of friends who prefer that.”
“If it suits you,” I answer.
“So why did they confine you to the dark room?”
“Is it so important to you?”
“I think we’d have a better understanding if we start with the most recent events.”
“I don’t feel like talking about it?”
He brings out a packet of Benson and Hedges cigarette from his attaché case and offers it to me.
“I don’t smoke,” I respond coldly.
“You quit smoking?”
“I never liked cigarettes.”

“Hmm,” he mutters nodding his head. He shrugs and throws the packet of cigarette into the briefcase, “Actually your case has been called for hearing twice, once in December, then in April, until it was adjourned for last hearing in July. We were contacted in the nick of time and filed an application for hearing to be postponed till August hoping that would give us enough time to look critically at the case…to see whether we’d accept to represent you…or not.”
“Who contacted you?”
“That shouldn’t be your interest for now Mr. Obiajulu,” he politely evades answering my question.
“Then I won’t talk if you don’t tell me who hired you.”
Segun nods his head in defeat, “I don’t know,” he says flatly and brings out a compliment card from his jacket and pushes it to me on the table.
I glance at it.

“Alpha Chambers, Victoria Island, Lagos. We usually don’t deal with suits like this, but I think my principal took a particular interest in your case and detailed me to take your deposition prior to the hearing. We are thirty-eight associates in all and we seldom have any interaction with the client at this stage.”

He sounds believable. I recall that at the same time he mentioned, two different lawyers on two different occasions had shown up sitting in that exact position and trying to talk me into letting them represent me in court. When I told them I couldn’t afford the fees they mentioned until I’m out of jail they went blank in the face, closed the cheap paper files they came with and left. Both of them never came back again after our first meeting.

I admire his black leather attaché case, a sharp contrast to the other two charlatans who took my deposition on a sheet of paper placed in a new office file which had my name and a case number printed boldly with a red marker. I can swear they threw their files into a bin as soon as our meeting was over.
“You must be a very lucky fellow to still be alive,” he says.
We glance at each other speechlessly.

“Well, since you’ve proven to possess an exceptional instinct to survive, I think if everything goes the way it should, you’d probably be setting the record of being the first man to be acquitted and discharged from Katampe prison since Mr. Sanusi took over as the prison warder,” he beams confidently as if he had the Judge’s word on that.
“What do you mean by if everything goes the way it should?”

Segun leans back on the chair and taps his pen on the table, then stands up uneasily and darts about the room. He’s probably about twenty-six and stands five feet eleven inches tall on a pair of one and half inch high-heeled black shoes he’s wearing. By my estimation, he hasn’t had much courtroom experience, but there’s an aura of confidence about him.
He comes back to his chair and scribbles something on his notepad and crosses his legs apparently waiting for me to speak.
“So, do you really think I’m innocent?”

“It doesn’t matter what I think Iyke,” he looks up at me and gives me a short smile, “Sincerely, after reading the statement you made at the police station the night you were arrested, I said to myself, this guy deserves a fair trial.”
I know if they’ve responded to the suit, it is definitely based on the statement I wrote at the police station in Jos, which is primary evidence, which is still fresh in my memory.
“She practically met me at the door while I was about leaving. I asked her to go with me but she said she’d rather wait for me?”
“Can you describe how she was dressed? Did she come with a bag, toiletries or any of other conveniences that may suggest she intended to spend the night with you?”
“She had her handbag, that’s all she had, a handbag. And don’t ask me what was inside, cause I didn’t bother to check,” I reply.
“So you can’t say precisely that she planned to spend the night?”
“I don’t know. I told you I didn’t invite her…she gave me a gift, which she brought from her handbag.”
“What kind of gift?”
“A gold wristwatch, expensive…a Rolex.”
“Where is it?”
I chortle. “The police took it.”
“You said the party took place outside the school premises, which particular hotel was that?”
“It wasn’t a hotel. It was a house party, in a friend’s bunk.”
“What’s his name?”
“Bitrus Shak.”

Segun quickly put down his name in his notepad. Although Bitty and I parted on a not-so-friendly note, I know he would cover me if he were called as a witness. Moreover, I bear no grudge against him for keeping aloof all this while.
He looks at his watch with a smile obviously satisfied with the information he’s gathered so far. I know this drill will continue a couple of times more before the case is finally over.
“One more thing Iyke…if the Judge finds you guilty of the offence you may likely face a death sentence…but there’s a way out.”

While I was staying in Uncle Dike’s house, I spent some time studying his law books. There are only two ways out, an out of court settlement with Sephia’s father, which is not likely to work, or a plea of guilt, which will leave me at the mercy of the Judge, in which case Segun will pray the Judge to commute the penalty of the offence for which I’m being detained.
“Don’t bother to mention it Segun.”
“Well, it’s only a matter of routine that I point this out and let you make up your mind to accept the judge’s verdict.”
“Am I not lucky enough to have a trial…I’ll accept whatever the Judge decides.”

He shakes his head. “I’m not making any promises, but as long as you cooperate with me, I’ll do the best I can to see that you get a fair trial,” he pauses, “And by God’s grace acquitted…if you’re innocent.”
I indicate my consent by raising my right hand with open palms.

He brings out the packet of cigarette, locks the black leather attaché case and comes to where I’m sitting. “Give it to your neighbours, a redemption gift. You’ll need their prayers too,” he says as he put the packet of cigarette in my breast pocket. “I strongly recommend a haircut. And shave your beards too. I’ll be back next week Tuesday okay.”
He rattles at the door for the security to open the door.
NINETEEN

The consequence of transgressing the laws of the GODs is severe, but my experience since I’ve been here has caused me to question the authority of the Order over my life. How could they have abandoned me this long? Against my will, I had my hair sheared and my beards cut off. For nearly five years, I’ve neither shaved nor barbed my hair, in compliance with the oath I swore as a star bearer.

The myth that trials are a mere formality has given me a complex about the judicial system – that the verdict is reached by the Judges ever before trials begin, especially if it is a murder charge, and if the accused person is detained at Katampe maximum security prison. Detainees arraigned from here are treated with barefaced prejudice.

In spite of that, I’m up against a principal odd, the ethnic and religious interplay of the characters involved in my trial. I am Ibo, and I’ll swear to be a Christian, the Judge, Justice Adamu Kachalla is a northerner, an Hausa, and a Muslim Mallam. Segun Babade is from the west, a Yoruba and seemingly a Christian. I’m not sure about Sephia’s religious inclination, but being a northerner, it may certainly stir a lot of sentiments and sway the judge’s verdict.

In any case, Segun tells me the choice of Justice Adamu Kachalla to preside over the case is a good omen. He describes him as one of the few reasonable and detribalised men in the bench. In addition, Segun reassures me that his tactical selection of his list of witnesses will douse any possible negative influence of such prejudice. I was a bit disturbed when he told me that Bitty, who he was banking on as a major witness, has travelled abroad for further studies. Nevertheless, his confidence in Justice Kachallah’s integrity has boosted my morale and prepared me psychologically for the trial, which is commencing today.

The wailing prisoners’ limousine, the Black Maria, swerves into the premises of Kaduna High court and stops in the full glare of a crowd who had gathered to catch a glimpse of the notorious criminal. Seven mobile policemen detailed to guard me carefully let me down the van. Bound hand and foot with chains, I wade through the crowd on my way to the court building.
“Hello Iyke, how are you today?” Segun greets me spiritedly.
“I don’t know.”
“It’s okay you’ll be fine,” he says smugly.
“I don’t want to be fine I want to be free.”
“May I introduce you to a senior colleague and associate, Frank Odikpo?”
“How do you do?” Frank Odikpo greets extending his hand for a handshake.
“Hello.” I hold my clasped hands down denying him a handshake.
Segun glances at the chains, and then turns to the policemen. “Who is in charge of his security?”
“You got any problem?” one of them replies.
“Yes there is. I will not have my client paraded as a criminal. I request you take the chains off him,” he demands.
“Is he not a criminal?” another policeman cuts in.

Segun turns to him. “Did you by chance come across the term, human rights violation act in the course of your training as a police officer?” They all stare sheepishly at Segun. “You don’t treat a law-abiding citizen as a criminal to the prejudice of his rights.”
“Take the chains off,” the most senior policeman of the rank of assistant superintendent of police orders them.
“How often do law enforcement officers break the law themselves?” Frank whispers to Segun. He looks at his watch and announces that hearing will commence at nine.

Segun Babade and Frank Odikpo are sitting in the front row facing the intimidating Judge’s seat on an elevated platform behind the clerk, a young man probably in his late twenties. He is arranging the files to set them in order as it appears on the course list. Out of reverence for the sanctum, footsteps of arriving guests are hardly heard as they quietly take their seats awaiting the appearance of the Judge. It’s only a matter of time before friends and relatives of litigants whose cases are slated for hearing today and a few zealous law students will fill the scanty gallery. Judging by the number of long benches, the gallery would accommodate close to two hundred people.

The prosecution counsel is in a brainstorming session with their old wigs lying on the table as they fine-tune their plot to sentence me to death! Surprisingly, my advocate is reading a Daily Times newspaper. I hope this is a strategy.
The clock on the wall appears blurred from dizziness and as soon as it is nine o’clock, I hear the sound of a knock on the door distantly, then the court clerk yells, “Court rise!”
The sound of his voice gives me goose pimples.
“Stand up Mr. Obiajulu, the Judge is entering,” Frank mumbles, bringing me back to the court.

The gallery rise to their feet rowdily. I join them. Justice Adamu Kachalla strides to his chair accompanied by two police orderlies carrying his briefcase. He bows to the bar and sits down. We all reciprocate by bowing our heads lightly. The police orderly put his briefcase on the table as he sits down, then we all sit quietly. An unsettling silence comes upon the room.
Justice Adamu Kachalla is in his mid forties. He looks serious in his black gown and long wig, which makes him appear overweight for his five feet six height. He patiently arranges his documents.
“May the court proceed,” he announces as he wear his gold-framed spectacles.
The clerk rises to his feet, facing the gallery and the bench, “Suit number HC/KD/106/1994, State versus Ikenna Obiajulu.”

I stand up to identify myself as he mentions my name. Two police orderlies lead me to the dock and make me stand. I understand I’ll be standing all through the trial unless I’m reported to be sick. For now, I’m healthy, and I hope I don’t fall sick.
“May the counsels in this case introduce themselves to the court,” the clerk demands.
“May it please my lord? Mr. Felix Ekun esquire for the State, with me are Mrs. Bimbo Eleru and Mr. Mike Okonta,” He responds smiling smugly at the Judge as he introduces his crew and then sits down.

Mr. Felix Ekun esquire has been in the bench for a long time, fifteen years. And he is accorded a lot of respect in the courts in the state. In contrast to my advocate’s few years of experience, Mr. Felix Ekun was called to bar twenty-two years ago, and has to his credit the death sentence of many criminals. Among his colleagues he is referred to as Ruthless General or simply Ruthless Gee. According to Segun, he is said to be the non-retiring attorney general of the state because he handles most of the State’s charges.
Frank Odikpo rises to his feet. “With due respect my lord, Mr. Frank Odikpo for the accused person, with me is Mr. Segun Babade,” he pauses, “Mr. Babade shall be taking the proceeding.”
“Mr. Ekun, what is the position of the suit?” Justice Kachalla asks Felix.
“My lord the plea of the accused has already been taken and subject to the court’s convenience, we are prepared to proceed with the trial today,” Felix replies with a smile.
“Mr. Segun!” Justice Kachalla calls.

The wooden table where my two-man counsel is sitting is either too close, too heavy to be moved or Mr. Ekun’s carriage has obviously affected Segun’s morale. He awkwardly flounders with the table as he rises to respond to the Judge. Frank lends him a hand shifting the table noisily. He let out a cough and quavers, “With due respect my lord, if the court is obliged to hearing us today, we are equally ready.”
“Mr. Ekun, how many witnesses do you intend to call?”
“Four my lord,” he replies.
“You may proceed with your examination.”
The Judge’s statement gets the adrenalin flowing.
“I am grateful my lord,” Felix replies holding a sheet of paper in his hand, “My lord the state wishes to call Mr. Akpan Peters into the witness box.”

Twenty-two year old Akpan Peters looks a lot older than the last time I saw him, the evening of the day Sephia was murdered. He is led by one of the court protocol into the courtroom and into the witness box.
The clerk comes to him to guide him in the oath swearing. “Are you a Christian or a Muslim?”
“I am a Christian,” Akpan replies.
He takes a Holy Bible and instructs him, “Place your hand on this Holy Bible and repeat after me. I, mention your name.”
“I, Akpan Peters,” he responds with his right hand on the Bible raising up his left hand with his palm open.
“Do solemnly swear.”
“Do solemnly swear,” Akpan repeats.
“That the evidence I shall give in this court.”
“That the evidence I shall give in this court.”
“Shall be the truth.”
“Shall be the truth.”
“The whole truth.”
“The whole truth.”
“And nothing but the truth.”
“And nothing but the truth.”
“So help me God.”
“So help me God,” Akpan concludes the oath recitation and sits down in the witness box.
The clerk turns to the Judge and announces, “My lord the witness has been sworn in with the Holy Bible in English language.”
“May the prosecution counsel proceed with the examination,” Justice Adamu Kachalla says leaning back on his chair placing his hand under his chin.
“Thank you my lord,” Felix replies as he stands up to examine his first witness, “Could you please tell this court your full names, address and occupation?”
“My names are Akpan James Peters, I live at number twenty-three Bauchi road Jos, and I am a final year accounting student in the Metropolitan University Jos.”
“How long have you been staying there?”
“Five years, right from my first year.”
“Do you know the accused person in this case?” Felix asks.
“Yes I do.”
“What is your relationship with him?”
“We used to be next-door neighbours.”
“What kind of building is number twenty-three Bauchi road?”
“It is a six-bedroom apartment house with a central kitchen at one end and a bathroom and toilet at the other.”
“And how close is your room to that of the accused person?”
“I stay a room away from him.”
“I suppose you guys see each other pretty often?”
“Yes we do virtually every day except during the hols,” Akpan replies and gives me a cold glance as Felix takes a pause putting down the sheet of paper he’s holding.
Although a few more visitors are strolling into the room, the court is gravely quiet.
“Cast your mind back to the events on the fourteenth of February nineteen ninety-three. Did you see the accused person that day?”
“Yes I did.”
“About what time was that?”
“I can’t say the exact time, but I estimate about one hour before the gunshot. He was coming from the bathroom while I was returning from school with my girlfriend.”
“Did you guys greet each other that night?”
“I did greet him…but he didn’t respond,” he replies.
“Can you describe his mood when you met him?”
“He was uh – he wasn’t in a very good mood.”
“Impressive, depressed, angry, can you give a description?”
Segun jolts from his seat and cries, “Objection my lord counsel is leading the witness.”
“Objection overruled,” Justice Kachalla replies coldly.
“He looked pale as if something was bothering him,” Akpan answers staring wickedly at me.
“Thank you Mr. Akpan. Now where were you at about ten o’clock that night?”
“I was right in my room.”
“Can you tell the court what you witnessed that night?”

Akpan adjusts himself inside the witness box and let out a cough to clear his throat and responds, “That day at about ten p.m., I was with my girl friend in my room when we began to hear the voice of a girl screaming. The noise was coming from his room,” he points at me and continues, “The screaming lasted about five minutes and then there was silence,” he pauses, “I heard the sound of the door open, then after the blast of a gun from the room.”
“Did you have any inkling of what was going on in the room?”
“Of course I knew the girl was being raped. It was rampant at that time, especially among fraternity boys. My girlfriend tried to persuade me to help her, to alert neighbours so as to rescue her.”
“Did you do that?”
“Mr. Akpan, do you realize you could have saved Sephia’s life if you had intervened that night?”
“I wish there was something I could do. But I was afraid,” Akpan replies frowning at me.
“Mr. Akpan, did you not feel any sense of responsibility to save the life of this poor girl? Shouldn’t you have at least alerted the neighbours as she suggested?”
Akpan fixes his gaze on me without answering him.
“What were you really afraid of Mr. Akpan?” Felix asks in a low tone of voice.
“I was afraid he could have a dangerous weapon. I was afraid of being shot,” Akpan replies.
“Mr. Akpan, the gunshot came after the murderer had left the room,” Felix says chuckling, “What could have given you the premonition that the intruder was in possession of a gun?”
“Well uh... most of them have pistols,” Akpan bleats.
“Them? Whom are you referring to as them, Mr. Akpan?” Felix quizzes him sternly.
“Secret cult guys,” the fool replies.

From the corner of my eye I catch Segun’s surprise glance at me. The subject about secret cult membership never came up at the deposition, and of course there was no hint about it in my statement. I won’t own up to it anyway, not even under an oath will I confess that I belong to a secret cult.
“That will be all for this witness my lord,” Felix submits.
Justice Kachalla takes notes as Felix takes his seat. “Mr. Segun!” He calls peeking at him from eyeglasses hanging on his nose.
“Thank you my lord,” Segun replies standing up facing Akpan at the witness box for cross-examination. “Mr. Akpan you said you have lived in number twenty three Bauchi road for five years, right?”
“Correct.”
“During the period, I mean prior to his arrest, has Ikenna Obiajulu ever been reported or accused of being involved in a rape?”
“Mr. Akpan you also said you heard the sound of the door open during the time of screaming?”
“Yes,” Akpan replies.
“After you heard the blast of gunshot did you hear the door open again?” Segun quizzes further.
“How long did it take you to leave your room after the gunshot?”
“About five minutes after the gunshot.”
“And you didn’t hear the door open again within that time?”
“And you didn’t by any chance see anybody enter or leave the room that night?”
“I did not,” he replies impatiently. Akpan is visibly uncomfortable in the box with Segun’s line of interrogation.
“Mr. Akpan have you ever been accused of raping a girl?”
Felix objects. Justice Kachalla sustains it. Akpan remains silent.
“You also said you couldn’t come to help the victim alleging the occupant to be a member of a secret cult,” Segun says smiling.
“I believe he –”
“Mr. Akpan,” Segun cuts in sharply, “The court is not interested in what you believe. Did you or did you not say that?”
“I did.”
Segun takes a pause then continues, “Mr. Akpan, do you have any idea how people become members of secret cults?”
“I believe they are initiated.”
There is chuckling in the gallery. Segun smiles and shakes his head.
“Order!” yells the clerk.
Segun stares at Akpan waiting for the noise to die down. “Mr. Akpan, were you present at the accused person’s initiation?”
“Certainly not,” Akpan replies sarcastically.

“Let me share your belief Mr. Akpan. I believe that there are peculiar attires or regalia and certain paraphernalia by which various cult groups are identified,” Segun takes a pause, “Have you ever seen Ikenna Obiajulu dressed in any such attire or regalia?”
“No,” Akpan answers with slight hesitation.
There is murmuring in the gallery.
“Order!” yells the clerk.
“Mr. Akpan what else do you know about the supposed secret cult which you allege the defendant is a member?” Segun asks staring furiously at him.
Akpan remains speechless fixing his eyes on him.
“I suppose your opinion about him being a secret cult member was based on hearsay,” Segun concludes.
There is brief silence in the court. Frank gazes at Akpan with his hands folded. He drops his head to the floor inside the witness box.
“I have no further questions for this witness my lord,” Segun submits turning to the Judge, then sits down. Segun and Felix glare at each other.
“Mr. Felix, any re-examination?” The Judge asks eyeing him.
“None my lord,” Felix replies.
“The witness may step down,” Justice Kachalla says facing Akpan at the witness box.
Akpan steps out of the witness box and a court orderly leads him out of the courtroom.
“My lord the prosecution would like to call its next witness, Miss Agatha Pepple,” Felix announces.

She strides gracefully into the courtroom dressed in a pink silk shirt and grey trousers. The chic perfume she’s wearing fills the air with a pleasant scent that quickly takes me to the drama that took place the day I first met her.

The day after my visit to Sephia’s room with Bitty, she met me at the cafeteria and told me a beautiful friend of hers is madly in love with me and asked her to deliver a message to me. I had never met Agatha before in my life, so I couldn’t guess who her friend could be. But I figured Agatha was playing matchmaker for her so-called beautiful friend. She handed me an envelope and vanished from sight. When I opened the card, the words, where will you be on fourteenth February, were handwritten on the inside page and there was no return address on the envelope.

That moment, I was curious and thought it would be such an intriguing game to play, but I was constrained to attempt to enter into a relationship with this stranger. Until now, I never set my eyes on Agatha again. It suddenly dawns on me it was Sephia who sent the card to me.
“Miss Pepple, do you know the accused person?”
“Yes, I got acquainted with him through my late roommate Sephia Rwang, her soul rest in peace,” Agatha replies.
“Amen,” Felix responds, “When did Sephia first tell you about her relationship with the accused?”
“I can’t remember the exact date, but he escorted a friend to our room a few days before the night she died.”
Agatha is lying. She wasn’t in the room the night Bitty and I visited their room and none of them, neither Bitty nor Sephia ever mentioned her name during our conversation that night.
“Did your roommate tell you anything about him, how she felt after they made acquaintance?”
“Objection my lord that is a leading question, I pray the court to strike out that question from the records,” Segun cuts in sharply rising to his feet.
“Objection sustained, Mr. Felix rephrase the question please,” Justice Kachalla responds.
“As the court pleases,” Felix replies and takes a pause, “Miss Pepple, What was Sephia’s relationship with the accused person at the time she first met him in your room?”
“Well, I’m not sure both of them had ever set eyes on each other before that day.”
“You’re sure about this?”
“Yep…actually it was Bitrus who was making advances on Sephia. He and Sephia had known each other way back.”
“Who is Bitrus?”
“His friend,” she points at me, “The one I said he escorted to our room.”
“I see,” Felix exclaims and takes a pause. Bimbo Eleru hands him a sheet of paper. He looks sternly at Agatha, “Miss Pepple when did you first hear about Sephia’s death?”
“I heard the news the day after.”
“How did you feel when you learnt that she died in the accused person’s room?”
“Objection my lord!” Segun barks.
“Objection sustained! Mr. Felix please.”
“Yes my lord,” he replies, “Where were you the night Sephia died?”
“Me?” she asks pointing at her chest.
“Yes, you Miss Pepple,” Felix reiterates.
“It was Valentine’s Day. Of course I was with my boyfriend,” she replies.
Felix takes a pause again and consults with Bimbo Eleru over something in the paper he is holding. Judge Kachallah looks at the time apparently impatient at Felix’s lumbering.
“My lord the counsel would at this juncture like to request that the said Mr. Bitrus be summoned to appear before the court as a principal witness.”

For obvious reasons Segun does not object. He told me how difficult it was for Bitty’s family to disclose information about his whereabouts. They’d probably be less wiling to assist Felix and his crew if they are told it has to do with a court case.
Judge Kachallah considers it for a while. “That brings the total number of prosecution witnesses to five.”
“Yes my lord, if I have your permission,” he replies politely.
“Granted!” Judge Kachallah consents.
“Thank you my lord, that’ll be all for this witness,” Frank concludes.
“Miss Agatha how close is your relationship with the deceased?” Segun asks.
“She’s my best friend.”
“She confides in you?”
“Yep,” she replies casually.
“Did she tell you if there was any prior appointment between herself and Iyke before the day she visited Iyke’s place?”
“There wasn’t.”
“You are sure there was no prior invitation to his room?”
“Yes I am very sure. She only hinted me while I was leaving the room that she wished she could be with Iyke that night.”
“So you’re not aware that she was going to his place that night?”
“No. I think she wanted to use the opportunity of the Valentine Day to stir up an affair with him, and she decided to pay him a surprise visit that night,” Agatha explains.
“I have no further questions for this witness my lord,” Segun concludes.
“Mr. Felix any re-examination?”
“Definitely my lord,” Felix replies springing up from his seat. “Miss Pepple what was your impression of the accused person the day he escorted his friend to your room?”
“Actually, I wasn’t in the room that night.”
“When did you first meet the accused?”
“I can’t remember precisely.”
“But I’m sure you can remember where you first met him.”
Agatha takes a deep breath. “It was on our way to the canteen one afternoon after Sephia had just finished telling me about Iyke and how much she’d like to date him,” she hesitates.
Felix watches Agatha with folded hands. The courtroom is unusually silent as if everyone is anxious to hear Agatha’s story.

“When we came to the canteen that day, there he was. While he was eating, we went to a nearby gift shop and bought a card,” she continues, “We planned that I’d deliver the card. Sephia asked me to deliver the card on her behalf…so I walked up to him and gave the card to him.”
“Can you recollect how long before the Valentine Day you met him at the canteen…three days…one week…two weeks…”
“Two weeks…I think so?”
“If the card was given to him two week before her visit, it probably wasn’t a Valentine card, was it?”
“No it wasn’t.”
“Now, assuming she didn’t take any other gift along on her visit, do you think the message in this card would appropriately convey her intention or affection for him?”
“Apart from the message printed on the card, she wrote a handwritten message that definitely suggests it.”
“What was the message?”
“Where will you be on February fourteenth?” Agatha replies.
“She wrote that in her own handwriting?”
“That’s a strange question considering that Sephia and the accused barely got acquainted with each other.”
“It was meant to be a puzzle…a game you know.”
“Knowing what the fourteenth of February stands for, I take that to mean that Mr. Obiajulu was actually expecting a date, on that Valentine Day.”
“Maybe,” Agatha replies.
Felix turns to the Judge and announces, “My lord, that’ll be all for the witness.”

The next prosecution witness is the prison warder, Mr. Sanusi. But he’s not in court. Felix tenders a written excuse to the Judge for his absence – that he is unavoidably absent because of his busy duty schedule at the prison. He promises to make him available at the next hearing.
I think the Judge was begging for it. He announces the case is adjourned to the thirty-first of August, and calls for the next case.
TWENTY

There has been an outbreak of cholera in the prison. After barely five days since the first patient was diagnosed, the killer disease has already claimed seven lives and left nearly a third of the population of inmates in severe condition. The environment is littered with food particles vomited by the infirm.

Harry hasn’t visited since the outbreak of the disease. I thought it was the fear of catching the bug that kept him away until I learnt the prison authority is turning visitors back. Because of their cavalier attitude to get medical aid for affected inmates, I suspect their reason is not to prevent visitors from contracting the disease, but to forestall the possibility of spreading a scandal about the outbreak that has reached epidemic proportion.

The privilege to leave the prison premises offers me some respite from the horrific experience here, and also the opportunity to get some medications for Steve. Thanks to Segun’s generosity, he helps me buy drugs prescribed by his doctor for the disease.
“So how did it go?” Steve asks in a lethargic tone. He was asleep when I came back from the court and I didn’t bother to wake him up.
“We just started…here.” I hand him a bottle of water.
“Thanks.”

Although it’s too early in my trial to begin to have expectations, Frank advised me to be very attentive to the proceedings and reflect on it after each session. He said it’d help prepare me for my testimony.
“I brought you some more drugs,” I whisper.

Steve gives me a pathetic glance. He looks pale and withered since he caught the bug. His hands tremble as he takes the drugs from me. “God bless his soul,” he gasps, and then he begins to cough. He rallies and says as he pants for breath, “You’re still going to be a doctor, aren’t you?”
“If they open a satellite campus in Katampe prison,” I reply jokingly.

Steve has been downcast since he fell ill. He confessed, however, that his health is worse off because of Harry’s long absence. I’ve been doing my best to keep him out of the doldrums by telling him jokes and comical stories.
“Harry was here today,” he snickers and starts coughing severely. “Will you still help me out of here if you’re discharged?”
“You’re so certain the verdict will go in my favour.”
“How’s your lawyer doing?” he wheezes.
“We just got started Mirage.” I pour water in a cup and make him take the drugs. “Well, my lawyer is good, but…”
“But what?”
“I have a feeling this is a mere formality. I think the judge has already decided what his verdict will be.”
“Don’t talk like that,” he says in a low tone.
“You know it’s true Mirage. You said it yourself. Has any prisoner ever been acquitted of a murder charge from Katampe prison?”
“Maybe I was wrong…your case is different Iyke. Harry’s optimistic about your acquittal.”
“What does Harry know about law?” I chuckle and ask sarcastically. “There are only two people who know that I’m innocent one of them is dead, and me…nobody believes me.”
“You’re leaving somebody out,” he says forcing a smile, “Me…I know you’re innocent Iyke.”
I take a quick glance at him.
“And your lawyer…if not he wouldn’t be defending you.”
I laugh. “He does that for a living. Supposing I actually killed Sephia, you think he wouldn’t still defend me for a good price even if he were watching me do it?”
“What are you saying?”
“What does he care? It doesn’t matter to him whether I’m guilty or not, he’s paid for the job he’s doing.”
Steve shakes his head without saying anything.
“Do you know what I’m planning Steve?” I whisper coming close to him, “I’ll get that golden opportunity one day and then…I’ll take off from there.”
“That’s an unnecessary risk my friend.”
“Wait and see.”
“You’ll be discharged,” he says with a tone of certainty.
“Is there anything you know that I don’t?” I ask.
He smiles and says, “Maybe.”
“Then tell me.”
“There’s somebody praying for you.”
I burst into laughter.
“Who is Julia?”
I suddenly cut off the laughter and frown at Steve. “You’ve been reading my diary.”
“Why would I do that?”
“Then how did you know about Julia?”

“Harry told me about her. Now don’t ask me how he got to know about her, I told you once you didn’t believe. He’s a man of God. God revealed some things to him about you…your father is a priest, isn’t he?”
Now this is serious. I lean on the wall squinting at Steve.
“And you are consecrated as a servant of God even before you were conceived, but you wouldn’t submit to Him…is this true?”
I do not respond.

“He said the Lord sent him to tell you He still loves you and cares for you and if you would humble yourself and pray, and if you are willing to turn from your wicked ways, He will hear from heaven and deliver you.”

I have never treated Harry’s words with any regard and I make no effort to hide it from him. But I’m overwhelmed by his profound knowledge of my secrets. Steve and Harry must have spent a great deal of time talking about me. He recounts Harry’s version of my childhood days as if he actually grew up with me, right in my neighbourhood.

When I was a little boy, I had a mysterious charisma. My friends, neighbours and close relatives used to tell me that I’d end up being a priest like my father. And because I was too young to decide what I would be in future, coupled with my father’s influence, I began to aspire for that office, but everything changed when I got into higher secondary school. Now, I’ve gone too far from God to return.

Harry is right in what he said about me, I used to enjoy unusual favour with people around me in those days, especially when I was chapel prefect, but all that changed when I left home... and left God.

One man that has such passionate negative disposition towards me is the prison warder, Mr. Sanusi. At first, I thought he was merely tribally prejudiced against me because he frequently curses me in his mother tongue, and threatened to blow my skull open should the judge acquit me of the charges in error. But, considering Harry’s revelation, I’m now convinced his repugnant attitude towards me is beyond ethnic prejudice.

I also know that although he has sworn in the name of his God, to tell the whole truth and nothing but the truth. I’m not expecting him to do that honestly. He’ll tell blatant lies under oath just to make sure I’m condemned to death.
Felix is in high spirits today and smugly dressed in the lawyer’s robe, complete with a wig. “Mr. Sanusi, how did you come to know the accused person?” he continues.
“He is an inmate in prison custody at the Katampe Maximum Prisons,” the warder replies speaking in his typical deep Hausa accent.
“Could you tell the court about the events of the tenth of April at the residence of Brigadier Zakari as it concerns the accused?”

“Well it is the practice within the prison system on occasions to go out with some prisoners for work outside the prison premises, this is a form of community service rendered by inmates from time to time, which is directly under my supervision,” he pauses and moves his body with exhilaration of a man of authority. “So on the tenth of April, the accused person along with seventeen other inmates were deported to the house of Brigadier Zakari for menial labour. He was detailed to repair a wardrobe since he had skills in furniture making. But instead of repairing the wardrobe, he wrecked it with the hammer he was given to work.”
The thought of the incident suddenly flashes on my mind and I feel a little dizzy and shut my eyes.
“So what happened after he destroyed the wardrobe?” Felix quizzes.
“It took a dozen men including armed soldiers to overpower him. He eventually fell on his knees and wept saying he was sorry that he didn’t know what came over him.”
“Was there any preceding events within the period that could have prompted such behaviour?”
“Of course,” he replies then he turns to me and our eyes meet. “This man is mentally imbalanced.”

“Objection my lord, the witness has not adduced evidence to suggest that he is a medical practitioner or psychologist. I submit therefore that he is not in a proper position to decide the mental state of the defendant,” Segun argues.

“Objection sustained, Mr. Felix kindly keep your witness within the confines of evidence within his knowledge and save us the unsolicited professional opinion,” Justice Kachalla responds, “You may proceed.”
Segun sits down and listens attentively to the Ruthless General leading his witness to tell lies against me. Justice Kachalla wearily listens to Felix with his chin resting on his hand.
After a drab thirty-five minute session, Segun takes over. “Mr. Sanusi, how long have you been a prison warder?” he asks facing the prison warder at the witness box.
“Sixteen years now,” he replies coldly.
“And during this long period of your service, I suppose you have seen many things and different kinds of people come in as your guests?”
“We do not entertain guests in the prison,” he snaps.
There is chuckling in the gallery.
“Of course we have had different kinds of people as inmates,” the warder continues.
“Thank you for your correction,” Segun replies calmly wearing a smile, “Mr. Sanusi, have you witnessed inmates on arrival to the prison breakdown and start crying for days?”
“I suppose in some cases some of them would refuse to eat food or drink water for days, especially newly admitted inmates?”
“Yes, we’ve had such cases too.”
“Have you ever tried to find out the cause of such behaviour, I mean the prison authority?” Segun quizzes.
“Yes, we have.”
“From your findings Mr. Sanusi, has this always occurred for the same reasons?”
“Could you tell this court some other reasons you discovered that was responsible for such behaviour?”

Mrs. Bimbo Eleru stares in bewilderment at the witness box, while Ruthless General confers with his associate, Mike Okonta. Mike Okonta shrugs and they break their conversation, and they both turn their attention to Segun’s cross-examination folding their arms.
“For some it was due to change of weather, some due to change of environment, some others due to stress and of course some we found to be mentally deranged,” he adjusts himself uneasily.

Chief Superintendent, as he likes to be called, is exasperated by the idea of sitting in a box and receiving a barrage of questions, questions which he must give an answer, from this young lawyer nearly half his age for that matter.
“So apart from being mentally imbalanced, there exist some other reasons to explain irrational behaviours of inmates?” Segun asks him.
Felix squints angrily towards the witness box.
“Yes,” he drawls angrily.
“My lord I have no further questions for this witness,” Segun concludes facing the Judge.
“Mr. Felix?” Justice Kachalla responds.
“I have no re-examination my lord,” Felix replies.
There are murmurings in the gallery as Justice Kachalla patiently takes note.

“For the purpose of clarifying the issue of the mental state of the accused, I demand that a proper psychoanalysis be carried out on the accused by a qualified and licensed psychiatrist. If I have the consent of both counsels, I recommend that a deposition of the accused person by a qualified psychiatrist be adduced on the twenty-seventh of September. The case is tentatively adjourned to the thirtieth of September.”
“As the court pleases,” they all chorus.
Justice Adamu Kachalla rattles the gavel to signal the end of the day’s sitting.
“Court rise!” the clerk shouts.
We all rise as the police orderlies usher the Judge out of the courtroom.

Felix leaves the court in a hurry escorted by Mike Okonta who is carrying his briefcase. Mrs. Bimbo Eleru carefully and slowly arranges her bag and joins the crowd noisily streaming out of the courtroom while Segun and Frank arrange their documents.
I am handcuffed and led away to the limo.
TWENTY-ONE

His right leg is crossed over the left and he’s wagging a shiny black shoe following the rhythm of the pen he’s tapping on the table. He’s letting out his fury. As soon as the prison attendants lock the door to the visitor’s room, the pen-tapping stops and the wagging foot is stilled.

The two lawyers representing me, Segun Babade and Frank Odikpo, have contrasting attributes, but it is a tremendous advantage in the proceeding. Segun is surly, exuberant and irascible, while Frank is calm and unruffled. If Segun were representing me single-handedly that old horse would have outwitted him. Frank had been in the profession long enough to realize how detrimental it is for a lawyer to blow his top during proceedings. Felix probably noticed Segun’s ill temper early enough and quickly devised the tactics of pushing him to the brink of losing his temper. Frank calms him down and restrains him from getting worked up at the prosecutor’s tailored derisive comments.

Frank Odikpo talks so slowly and with few words that, at first, I wondered why he chose the legal profession. He was called to the bar nearly fifteen years ago, at the age of twenty-four. His face is constantly etched with a dim-witted smile that smothers the enormous acumen he’s gifted with.
“Good afternoon Frank…hi Segun.”
“Hello Iyke. I hear the disease has reached epidemic proportions?” Frank replies.
Segun silently beckons me to a seat. I take the seat facing him.
“I pray you don’t catch the bug?” Segun says coldly.
“If I’m lucky to be evacuated before the case is over,” I reply.
“Well, we won’t be the one to stand in your way, neither will it be the Judge,” Segun replies, “Your acquittal in this case lies in your hand, Mr. Obiajulu.”
I glance at Frank and turn to Segun speechless.

“The surest way for a defendant to lose his case is to lie to his advocate,” he says in a flat angry tone. Then he put down his leg and brings out a sheet of paper from his briefcase. “Read this,” Segun says handing the paper to me, “I believe you know what that is?”

I do. The paper is a copy of my statement at the police station in Jos, the night I was arrested. I hand it over to him after silently reading through it. He takes it from me and brings another sheet of paper from the briefcase. This is a copy of his deposition before my trial began.

“These are two statements you made at different times…in different circumstances,” he takes a pause, “I may not have practiced law for so long, but an older colleague,” he looks at Frank who responds with a short smile, “made me understand that the truth doesn’t change with time…nor is it affected by the circumstance in which it is told…there’s something wrong with your alibi Mr. Obiajulu,” he says.

“It would have gone unnoticed,” he continues, “Thanks to Frank who meticulously compared the statements and raised eyebrows about the slight difference in the venue you stated that the party took place that night. You said you left Sephia in the room to attend a party…where did the party take place, which hotel?” Segun asks.
Frank crosses his legs, but his hand is still under his chin. I won’t be surprised if they leave me without him uttering another word.
“It was a house party, at a friend’s house.”

“A house party,” he giggles, “That’s the version of the story you told me, but your statement at the police station reads that the party took place in a hotel. So which are we to believe, which one is the truth? Did the party take place in a hotel or was it a house party?”
I blush and remain silent.

“You know what Mr. Obiajulu, none of those are true. You did not attend a party that night, neither in a hotel nor at a friend’s house,” he says with clenched teeth. He bends down and looking into my eyes says, “Frank went to your school to double-check on the facts.”
“I was away at a –”
“Oh, will you shut up!” Segun snaps angrily.
“Does the name Cyclone ring a bell?” Frank asks softly.
My heart misses a beat. Segun sits down and leans back on the chair.
“What did you discuss with him?” I ask Frank.
“It isn’t necessary,” Frank drawls, “As far as he’s concerned, you’re history. I don’t know how they got the gist that you died in the prison…he said you deserved to be in hell.”

Me dead! How? I picture Cyclone expounding the details of the hit to him, spicing every detail to prove that I truly deserve to be condemned to hell. He probably has been told how I brutally crucified the guy in the pub that night.
Frank gives me a puzzled stare betraying the deep-seated fear within. “Were you really so evil to deserve that?” Frank asks.
“I didn’t kill her,” I whisper. “You’ve got to believe me.”
“That’s not the problem,” Segun answers.
“You’re not going to abandon me now, are you?” I plead.

“Not if you’re willing to cooperate. Our case would have been jeopardized if I submitted this copy of your deposition,” he says waving the paper in the air, “Frank noticed it early enough and advised me to expunge it from our response…so we have a little secret between us. I trust we can keep it.”
“Why did you do that?” I ask him.
“Because we are convinced you didn’t kill Sephia,” Segun replies, “At least we know what kept you away from your room that night.”

“We already know what the prosecution is playing at, to convince the Judge that you’re guilty on the premise that you have the propensity of committing the crime by establishing the fact that you’re a secret cult member,” Frank adds, “But as far as we know Judge Kachallah, he’s not so daft as to fall for such frivolity.”
“What if he plays to Felix’s argument?” I ask.

“That’s our business. To make sure he doesn’t,” Segun giggles, “In fact that’s what informed the idea of going to Metropolitan…to find out if you really were a cultist,” he pauses, apparently expecting me to comment on it. I remain silent.
“Cyclone confirmed that a certain cop had one of his men interrogated about the hit and about your group,” Frank adds.
I exhale heavily and lean back on the chair.

Segun leans forward and says in a subdued tone, “Mr. Obiajulu, you’re not on trial for being a member of a secret cult but for the murder of Sephia Rwang, does that make sense to you?” Segun says quietly.
“So how bad is our case?” I ask in a low tone.
“We have the edge and Felix is already aware of that,” Segun replies.
“We got nothing Segun, can’t you see that. This is hopeless,” I reply droopily.
“You’ve got to trust me Mr. Obiajulu,” he says apparently losing his temper, “us,” he gasps, “We don’t have anything short of what they have against you.”

“Felix’s style is to build his argument around unsuspecting singular evidence, and then like a scavenger, he explores it fully pursuing it until he creates a loop hole. You can give him that or you can deny him, Mr. Obiajulu,” Frank says and leans back on his chair.
“Your meeting with the shrink is for Tuesday,” Segun informs me handing me a sheet from his legal pad.
I take the paper from him and stare at it with astonishment. It’s a letter from the psychiatrist manually typed on a cheap letterhead legal paper.

“The prison warder has given him his usual scaffold by stating carelessly that you’re neurotic,” Frank explains further, “Going by his past records, he’d want to reinforce that argument, and that will be his waterloo.”
“You think he predisposed the prison warder to do that?”

“Not exactly,” Segun replies, “Frank stumbled on a stunning revelation while going through Felix’s previous cases. In nine out of ten murder cases he prosecuted, he had always succeeded in establishing that the crime may have been induced by a psychological disorder and gotten the Judge to refer the accused to a psychiatrist.”

“Our profession requires some technicalities too, and it seems this is Felix’s individual strategy, even though it is not acceptable. Surprisingly, from my investigation, I discovered that he won all the cases that followed that course,” Frank says, “Judge Kachallah himself had presided over two of such cases. But I noticed something interesting about his choice of psychiatrist…it had always been the same shrink, Doctor Dalhatu Idris.”
“But you have a right to object,” I blast.

“We are guided by some professional ethics, Mr. Obiajulu. If we contest it, Felix could term it denigration. But if what we are thinking is true, it may be our golden opportunity to turn the case around,” Frank explains further smiling smugly,

“Now listen Mr. Obiajulu,” Segun breaks in, “We want you to be very careful and alert when answering Dr. Dalhatu’s questions. It’s not really what you say that matters to him, because shrinks listens, not to his patient’s stories, which are often lies, but to the voice of their conscience.”
“So what kind of question am I expecting?”

“Usually their routine questions bother on inquiries about the patient’s childhood patterning, relationship with the opposite sex, past life of violence and all that stuff. Like I said, it is not from the information he gets that the psychologist arrives at his inference, but your reaction, your mood to his questions. But, since an evidence of a violent behaviour already exists…you know what I mean?”
I know he’s referring to the wardrobe issue.
“He may tow a slightly different angle so you’ve got to be careful how you respond to his query,” he continues.
“So, should I tell him the truth then?” I ask him.
Segun and Frank eye each other.
“I hear some people say the truth shall set you free, but professionally speaking, I really don’t believe there’s an absolute truth about any matter in life,” Frank concludes.
“So what am I supposed to do?”

“I like the naivety in that question,” Frank comments with a smile, “That is expected of a first-time visit to a shrink. You’ll do well not to let him become suspicious of your attitude. Don’t appear to feel too confident about his questions. Just be yourself okay.”
I’ll try.

Except for an old shingle on the door leading to Dr. Idris’s two-room office apartment, there is no signboard to suggest that there’s a business activity going on in the three-story residential quarters located in the suburbs of Kaduna.

The female clerk is fast asleep and snoring, from the doldrums of bad business, I guess. She excuses us and enters the next room to inform her boss about our arrival. The cramped, drab reception has no chair for visitors so we are kept standing for a while before the shrink let us into his office.

I try to remember all the instructions I received from Segun and Frank, especially the story I told the cops to be sure it agrees with whatever I’ll be telling Dr. Idris. I have a feeling Felix would have furnished him with the testimony to detect whether I’m lying or not.

His office is hazy from a smouldering cigarette placed in an ashtray. He greets us grudgingly swivelling gracelessly in the chair, beckons me to sit down and rudely asks my security escorts comprising of a prison security officer and two policemen to wait for me at the reception. They’ll be standing.
Fumes from the stub of cigarette in the ashtray irritate me, but I’ll put up with it. Dr. Idris looks like a man who likes to have his way.
“So how’s the court proceeding going, do you think you’re having a good trial?” he asks after writing my name in a tattered old notebook.
I remain silent.
He looks up and says with a smile, “Well, aren’t you lucky? Justice Kachallah is one of the best on the bench, hard nut to crack.”
I sneeze from the irritation of the smouldering cigarette, but he ignores me.
“If I am rightly informed you pleaded not guilty. That’s your lawyer’s way of saying the State is wrongly accusing you of a crime you didn’t commit, is that right?”
“Certainly.”
“Anyway it’s the responsibility of the lawyers to discover the truth, isn’t it?”
I remain silent.
“So let’s start with hearing your own side of the story, what actually happened that night?”

I recite the account of the incident as I told the police. After he asks what Segun described as the routine questions, about my family, of course I didn’t tell him the problem I have with my father, that’ll give me away. Then he asks about my past relationships if I had been involved in any act of violence. Nope. It’s a blind alley so he goes back to the reason why I’m detained.
“You must have had a good reason to let her stay back in the room, like – maybe you had another date or something,” he says leaning forward, “Was that really the reason?”
“But you do have another girlfriend don’t you,” he asks.
“I didn’t.”
He writes it in the notebook. “Oh yes I remember you said your father is a priest. I guess you were constrained because of your religious beliefs,” he giggles.
“It is not on account of religion that I abstain from women.”
He looks up from writing with a grimace.
“Do I necessarily have to be a Christian just because my father is a priest?” I pause and gaze at him to figure out what’s on his mind, “I don’t really believe in religion.”

I noticed when we came in there’s nothing around the office that suggests his faith, not any of the common articles like almanacs having religious dates, or picture of a holy being, or a priest, or any of those things by which one can be associated with a particular faith. The walls are drab, blank and without any decorations.
“You’re an atheist?”
“I didn’t say I don’t believe in God.”
“Then you’re a heathen?”
“Nope.”
“Anyway, let’s leave that,” he sighs and quickly drops his eyes on the notebook.

“If what my father said about God is true, that He’s all-knowing, He should have vindicated me…because I’m innocent,” I say in a low tone, “I stopped believing in Him since I was arrested for a crime I didn’t commit.”
“That’s why we have lawyers to prove that you’re innocent…beyond reasonable doubts,” he says.

During his disposition, I was expecting Dr. Idris would ask me about my affairs in school, precisely about any involvement with the secret fraternities. But when the session ended without a mention of it, I became suspicious. He should have known that nearly one in ten guys on campus today are members of a confraternity, and more than half of the civilian population have had a brush with a cultist over a girl who they mutually admire.

Whatever the case might be I was of the opinion that the possibility of associating with the fraternity should have been precedent in his line of questioning, and I had my mind prepared for his questions. But listening to Felix’s examination of the shrink this morning, I think, in Frank’s words, I have given him the scaffold to build his argument. Now I understand why the cult issue never came up during his deposition – he concluded that since I’m an atheist, I had the proclivity of being a cultist.
If what I’m thinking is right about these two crooks, Felix will follow that line to argue that there’s no reason why I couldn’t have been a member of a cult.
True to his style, and apparently to the chagrin of the Judge, Felix has been drivelling with Dr. Idris for nearly half an hour on this.

“Please confirm that this is the copy of your report,” he says holding a sheet of paper in his hand. The clerk reluctantly goes to Felix, takes the paper from him and gives it to Dr. Idris in the witness box.
“Yes, this is the report I submitted to the prison authorities,” he replies after browsing at the paper.
“My lord we wish to tender this document in evidence,” Felix says.

The court clerk brings the document to Segun, he and Frank peruse it. Segun whispers something to Frank and stands up. “My lord, we have no objection to the admittance of the report in evidence, but we shall be contesting the weight to be attached to it by the court.” He gives the paper back to the clerk and sits down.
“Psychoanalytic report dated twenty-seventh September nineteen ninety-four is hereby admitted in evidence and marked exhibit A,” Justice Adamu Kachalla responds.
“Now, Dr. Idris could you give us a breakdown in a layman’s terms of what is contained in your report,” Felix asks facing Dr. Idris.

“The report simply states that the accused person is neurotic, which means that he is given to unprovoked violent outbursts in the course of which grievous physical harm may be done to him or any other person around him at such a time.”
“Could such a state of mind cause him to shoot or kill another person?”
“Most certainly,” Dr. Idris replies.

The session looks more like a scene in a theatre than a court examination, as if the duo is playing out an act that apparently had been rehearsed earlier, and it has gotten the Judge’s eyebrows raised. After an hour and ten minutes, the curtain is finally brought down on their drama.
“Mr. Segun,” the Judge calls Segun for cross-examination.
“Thank you my Lord,” Segun responds standing up with gusto, “Dr. Idris, how long have you practised as a licensed psychiatrist?”
“Nineteen years,” Dr. Idris replies.
“Giving your long period of practice, I’m sure you have come across the term evidence of system?”
“Yes,” Dr. Idris replies.
“Can you explain to the court what this phrase means?”
“It is used to represent a situation where an individual conducts himself in a particular manner so much so that it is acknowledged as the person’s standard form of behaviour,” Dr. Idris replies.
“Now considering that the accused person on whom you conducted psychoanalytic test had only on one occasion displayed such erratic behaviour, would that suffice to establish an evidence of system?”
“In the case of neurotic disorder such a principle does not apply,” Dr. Idris replies.

“Dr. Idris, I would like to remind you that you are under oath, and any false information given by you would amount to perjury, punishable under the criminal code,” Segun sternly tells him with a gruff.
“Objection my Lord!” Felix stands up in protest, “The witness is aware he is under oath, counsel is threatening the witness.”
“Objection overruled.”
“It is my profession and I know what I am saying,” Dr. Idris responds.
“I have no further questions my Lord,” Segun concludes glaring at him.
Felix scowls at Segun as Dr. Idris is led out of the witness box. He’s the last witness on the prosecutor’s list.
TWENTY-TWO

At eight-forty on the dot, the prison limo enters the court premises and I am escorted into the court building by a band of armed policemen for the defence session, which will commence, as usual by nine o’clock.

Segun tells me my trial is gathering a lot of attention locally. I’m least impressed. He says it is tagged the trial of the most dangerous cultist. Is it in the news? Not yet he says. I hope the publicity doesn’t go beyond this. I don’t want the news to reach my father.
“Guess who our first witness is?” he asks.
“Don’t tell me Sephia has resurrected,” I reply putting the three of us in a lighter mood.
“The dean of the faculty of biological sciences, Professor Aliu,” Segun announces.
“What!” I exclaim, “Why did you have to get him involved in this shit?”
“At this stage, we needed to work on your personality. Frank came up with the idea of bringing in one of your lecturers and invited him as a witness,” he says grinning.
Segun convinces me of the importance of Professor Aliu’s testimony. He says he spoke well of me and happily accepted the invitation to come and witness.
Judge Kachallah flags off the proceedings and invites both counsels to respond.

“With the utmost respect of the court, Frank Odikpo for the accused and with me is Segun Babade who will take the proceedings.” Being Segun’s senior in the bar, he formally introduces the defence counsel to the court.
“What is the position of the case?” Judge Kachallah replies.
“My lord,” Segun responds as he stands up to address the Judge, “The case was adjourned till today for defence and if the court is prepared to hear us today, we are ready.”
“How many witnesses do you have, Mr. Segun?” Justice Kachallah demands.
“Just four my lord,” Segun replies spiritedly.
“You may proceed.”
