But somehow I came to the conclusion that for some it’s beyond what is said about the leopard’s skin, it’s in the gene.”
“Hey, my father is a decent man.”
“I know…bad son of a good man,” he chuckles.
“Anyway I don’t do that anymore.”
“I die! Has school changed you that much?”
“So what else do you do apart from shooting people in the dark?”
He chuckles. “Well, I’m pretending to be reading history in the polytechnic and this is my second year at it. How about you?”
“I’m in chemistry, and I’m not pretending about it.” We laugh it off.
“You know, I never thought you’d still make it into the university.”
“What are you implying, that I’m a dullard?”
“Not that. I thought you’d join your father in business. So why haven’t you blended?”
“Blended,” I reply, “How are you so sure I’ve not?”
“Hard men are known by their fruit, my friend,” he says.
“If shedding blood is all you people do in the fraternity, count me out Paul,” I reply sarcastically.
“If you’re referring to what happened yesterday, we didn’t intend that anybody should die. It was only meant to be a message,” he replies.
“A message?”
“Yeah, a message. Sorry, you won’t understand these things if you’re not in the system.”
“Well, that was obviously a costly assumption, only I wonder why it had to be the chick and not Steel.”
“We don’t leave the blind target to chances. Apart from the girl’s death, the hit was successful…unless you’re judging it by your affair with the chick.”
“I wasn’t dating Emem.”
He glances at me. “You obviously have a lot of questions you want to ask. Meanwhile, how about your rival, the cripple, have you seen him lately?”
When our eyes meet, he winks his right eye at me. I shake my head and turn away.
“I think for now you should be concerned about him,” he says.
“You’ll need more than your pistol to fight him my friend. I hear they’re already counting your steps.”
“Counting my steps?”
“What I mean is you’re under their surveillance twenty-four-seven. You need to protect yourself from them now or it’d be too late.”
“You’re not trying to insinuate me into your group, are you?”
He glances at me and smiles.
“You know after the escapade called palm fronds. I swore not to have anything with the fraternity.”
“This is different, man. I belong to a very wealthy and powerful organisation. If you become one of us, you’ll be so rich and powerful.”
“I’ve heard that many times from about three scouts of campus cults. Don’t you guys have another line?”
“I see you’ve resorted to operating solo. Well, let me tell you cowboy, you don’t know what you’re up against with Steel. He won’t spare you.”

Initially, I had considered Jshap’s prediction about Steel’s retaliation to be inconsequential, but going by what Paul is saying, I think I’ll be making a terrible mistake sweeping it under the carpet. He’s not a novice in gangland, neither am I.
“You know what Paul…I think you set me up at the party.”

He breaks up. “Well, I didn’t. I actually saved you. You would have been worse off if I hadn’t taken the pistol off you. Otherwise, how could you have denied having anything to do with us,” he explains.
“So how do we clean up the mess?” I ask him.
“There’s somebody I’d like you to meet. In fact I had to do something urgent when I heard they’ve already started making plans to hack you down.”
“I suppose that’s where you’re taking me?”

Barrister Ilaje lives in the New Marina garden city, a newly developed estate, not just for the rich and high echelon of the society, but the aesthetes. The estate is situated on a reclaimed sandy beach soil. It is crammed with modern-day architecture and the streets are dotted with state-of-the-art automobile.

The compound is cramped and as expected, bereft of flora. He and his wife are sitting outside in the veranda. He instructs the steward who received us to show us the way up to the veranda. After exchanging pleasantries, his wife excuses herself to arrange for refreshments, I guess.
“Is this the young man you told me about?” Chief Ilaje asks.
“Yes Chief,” Paul replies.
“What is your name?”
“Ugochukwu Amanze, sir,” I answer.
“You’re welcome Ugo.” We shake hands the second time. “You have a firm grip, you’re not as soft as you appear,” he says with an intention to amuse me.
“You’re right Chief,” Paul responds.
“What course are you studying?” Barrister Ilaje asks me.
“Chemistry, I’m a first year student.”
“First year?”
“Yes sir.”
“I see.” Barrister Ilaje eyes Paul with a squint of disappointment.
“Chief, Ugo and I have known each other for a very long time, and uh – we have done a lot of things together. He’s the man we need Chief. Trust me.”
The man we need? What the hell do they need me for? Paul never mentioned anything to me on our way to this place, except that this man is a lawyer and that he’s going to help me.
“Did you discuss with him?” he asks Paul.
“No,” Paul replies looking directly at him.

Barrister Ilaje holds himself back and takes a long stare at me as the steward serves us drinks. He motions Paul to him as soon as the steward leaves us and whispers something in his ears. Paul whispers back to him. What the hell is going on here?

Chief Ilaje clears his throat. “Well Ugo, I would have asked that we reschedule this meeting, but since you’re already here I’ll do you the honour of letting you know why Paul contacted you…I will then allow you to make up your mind,” he takes a pause, coughs again and steals a quick glance at Paul.
Paul nods slightly to show approval.
“Let me ask you a question Ugo. Have you ever desired something so badly, so desperately that you could do anything to have it?”

I glare at Paul. He put his head down staring at the floor with folded arms. Paul has obviously told this man about my relationship with Lizzy – if his intention is to take advantage of that to lure me into whatever they are planning, I won’t give in to it. He’s aware that I’m watching him and he’s deliberately refraining from looking in my direction.
“No,” I reply.
“Paul told me you once had a flair for the black arts, is that true?” he takes a fleeting look at Paul.
“No. He probably is mistaken.”
Paul turns sharply to me in surprise. I ignore him.

When we were pardoned and readmitted back into school, after I met Jshap and he thought me how to do the magic art, everyone in school began to admire me and treat me with great respect, including my teachers. I bragged about using my power to influence the principal to rescind the decision to expel us after Jshap told me about his visit to the principal’s office.

I refused to share the secret source of my power with any of my friends and lied to them that I conjure up money. My friends got excited and persuaded me to do it, but when I went back to persuade Jshap to teach me how to do it, he refused and asked me to give it up. When I went back to school, they began to deride me calling me a liar. But my interest in magic died out when Jshap left the house.

“It's okay Paul,” Barrister Ilaje says calmly, “Well Ugo, I will go ahead to tell you why I sent for you,” he hesitates, “There is a book, which is referred to as the heart of the gods... superior to any material of magic. Nearly half a decade ago it was declared missing, but recently, I gathered from a reliable source that the person who has the book is right here in Lagos.”
“Chief,” I chuckle, “there are more than five million people living in Lagos, that’s like looking for a needle in a hay stack.”

“Your chance of finding the book is narrower than you think. He’s a member of the group that was attacked at the party yesterday, so your mission is not as difficult as it appears, and if you are willing, you’ll have two million Naira as your reward and some other privileges, which we will discuss later if you succeed in bringing the book to me.”
Paul and I eye each other.

“Naturally, the book ought to be in the custody of the head of the group, but the former custodian of the book disappeared mysteriously. First, we need to plant an infiltrator in the group, who will eventually take over as the head, then –”
“Then he’ll become the custodian of the book,” I snap completing the sentence. “Well, you know what Chief, first of all I think the hit in the hotel was nasty,” I snarl.
“You are right,” Chief Ilaje concurs, “But it was one of the stages of the plan. That plan has been aborted because of the problems we encountered.”
“Paul said it was meant to be a message, what does that mean?” I quiz him.

“You’ll get to know later on,” he says smiling, “Paul called me this morning to tell me he’s got another idea and told me about you. Let me also state that my offer is subject to negotiation…if you let me know you’ve located the book.”
“So you’re sending me on an espionage mission to the group?” I inquire.

“It’s not as dangerous as you may think if you’re considering the American or British experience with the KGB. As a matter of fact, you really are not working for anybody,” he explains. “Paul will be completing his diploma program this year, if you’re able to accomplish this mission you’ll automatically be admitted into our prestigious fraternity. You’ll have much to gain as the group’s Capone and become the most powerful man on campus, and you’ll make your rival Steel look like a choirboy.”
I heave and brace myself. “What’s it about this book?”
“The book,” he hesitates, “Like I said earlier, it’s the heart of the gods. Nothing will be impossible for anyone who can unlock its secret code.”

This is a dangerous mission, but an adventurous one too. If this book really exists, it may be that I’m destined to be the one to have it. But what if Steel eventually discovers I’m working for them…I could be killed. On the other hand, if I tell this man I’m not interested, I could be eliminated for merely hearing this secret plan or even knowing who they are.

Did Jshap have a premonition of this? Is this really what he meant when he mentioned devil’s alternative. I’ll keep them in limbo until they forget we ever had this discussion. “I’ll think about it, Chief,” I mutter.

“We’re running out of time. The initiation of new members into the group is coming up in a fortnight, and they’re already selecting prospective candidates to be recruited. I’m afraid this is about the only chance we have to break into them,” Paul coaxes me.
I exhale heavily as I weigh the options before me. I catch Paul from the corner of my eye nodding to him.
“For security reasons we don’t want to expose you any further than this. You will make no further contact with Paul. I’ll send somebody to you tomorrow. Paul, show him how he’ll recognize him.”
Paul and I shake hands the way of the fraternity.

“You will get to meet the other members of our prestigious family when you return with the book and until then you will not see me again,” Barrister Ilaje adds, “We will send somebody to you when we perceive you are having any problems. Is that understood?”
“Yes sir.” I shake hands with him – the same way I did with Paul. And just like that, I suppose, I’ve been initiated into this nameless group.

On our way from Barrister Ilaje’s house, Paul furnishes me with details about the hit. The mission was codenamed Operation Sundown. The word blind target that Jshap mentioned earlier re-echoes when he explains the intrigue employed in spotting Steel in the dark. The deft logistics involved in the attack and the marksmanship gives me some impression of the kind of people I’m dealing with. Up till now no one has been able to identify the group or associate any likely student to its membership. Except me, of course, I’m the only one suspect Steel and his men know.
When we get close to Steel’s place, I ask him to drop me off.

Sammy Briggs, Steel’s friend, answers the door and bluntly tells me to go away, but I insist on seeing him. When he hears I have a message for him, he calls Sammy from inside the room and asks him to let me in.
He is lying in bed with a pistol in his hand. They are visibly agitated at my unexpected visit. “What brings you to my crib, my enemy?” Steel asks.
“I’m not your enemy…I have some information that may interest you about what happened yesterday,” I begin on a firm tone. “Something I noticed.”
“Are you here to betray your group?” Sammy chuckles.
“I don’t belong to any group and I don’t have anything to do with the attack. You don’t know why Lizzy wasn’t at the party yesterday, do you?”
They squint suspiciously at me.

At this precise moment considering the look on their faces, they wouldn’t believe me if I tell them what exactly happened to Lizzy, so I told them that she fainted on our way to the hotel and I only came to fetch Emem so we could take her to a doctor when I bumped into the fracas.
“Rubbish!” Sammy barks, “He came to exonerate himself.”

“That’s not why I’m here. I noticed something and I think it’d help you figure out the group that stormed the party. But first I want you to promise me that you will protect me if I give you this information.”
Both of them eye each other. Sammy replies, “Protect you, from who?”
“From them…I mean whoever they are.”
“We don’t run a security outfit here. If you think your life is threatened, go to the security department okay,” says Sammy.
Steel stops him by the wave of his hand.
“Let’s put aside our differences Steel. Do you think I’d be as stupid as to betray my group if I was one of them? Turn the light off.”
“What?” Sammy exclaims.
“You heard me, I said turn the light off,” I repeat firmly. I raise my hands and stand up to show that I’m unarmed.
Sammy eyes Steel and they both look askance at me. Steel cocks his pistol, points it at me and gives Sammy the go-ahead by nodding his head slightly. At the flick of the switch the room becomes dark.
“Can you tell what time it is Steel?” I ask him.
The glowing luminous reflector in his watch follows him as he raises his left hand.
“What do you see?” I ask rhetorically. “Now wear the light.”
Sammy flicks the light switch again.
“This is what happened at the party. I noticed it was your watch that gave them the lead to your position.”
They both eye each other.
“So how did you figure it out?” Sammy inquires.

When Paul told me about this, I thought that if I told them, it’d douse their suspicion that I belong to the group and pave the way for my initiation into the group. They did buy into it hook line and sinker. Although Sammy’s apparently not completely convinced, Steel is elated with the piece of information.
“So it wasn’t really a blind target after all,” he says in a low tone.
Sammy doesn’t respond. His face is etched with a suspicious expression.
“Do you know the group that did this?” Steel asks.
“You’ll be the first to know if I hear anything about them. But I want you to give me your word.”
“Well, about your request, there’s only one way we can protect you…have you thought about it?”
“Yes I have.”
“What do you think, Sammy?”
“I think he’s a traitor, Steel…he’s insinuating his way into us.”

“I wouldn’t have bothered myself with what is happening between you and whoever it is that attacked you yesterday, I’m only concerned about the rumour making rounds that I’m a member of that group,” I take a pause, “I’m not here to convince you about my connection with the group, but to ask you to get your men off my tail.” I stand up to leave.
Steel and I shake hands, but Sammy declines, instead, he opens the door for me to leave.
THIRTY-SIX

Based on my personal observation, by spotting guys who hang out with Steel and Sammy, I notice that the fraternity is a blend of people of different calibre, attributes and attitudes – the indigent and indignant, the prosperous and proud, and the plucky and creampuffs. And even a midget like Steel could head such a powerful group, and he’s dreaded for that matter. Observing their association from outside, I find the secrecy of their fraternization unusually interesting.
By my personal observation also, I’ve been able to identify about twenty-seven guys who I suspect to be likely members of the cult group. But, how could this one have escaped my notice?

My contact is escorted by another quiet fellow who I see around campus regularly. Pretending to be shaking hands with me, he slips a tiny black coffin-shaped card into my palm, and in a gruff tone, he instructs me that nobody else must see the card, and if possible, I should hide it from God. He also warns me not to come near the rendezvous if for any reason I lose the card. Then they fade away as unnoticed as they had come.

Because of the size of the card, I hid it inside one of the books in the shelf to avoid misplacing or losing it. I got the hint that hazing into the fraternity could be extremely physical so I wear a black turtleneck shirt made of thick viscose and a black jeans. But in order to avoid raising eyebrows on my way to the rendezvous, I don a brown jacket over the shirt and lace up my boot.

Jshap deliberately blocks the door when I’m about to leave after dressing up. His giant figure leaves no room for me to pass. He’s been very suspicious of my movement recently and he’s been wearing a pale expression for the past three days. He bluntly told me yesterday that he strongly senses that I am being tempted to join the cult and that I obviously have started tending towards the fraternity. But I denied it. If he thinks he can stop me from attending the initiation, he’s got to be kidding.

Apart from my concern about the prolonged detachment between Lizzy and I, I anticipated Jshap would interfere with my affair with the fraternity so I rented another apartment. Lizzy hasn’t been to my crib again since her first, after the night of her party. Her frequent inquiry about Jshap each time I go to visit her tells me he’s the reason why she’s refusing to pay me a visit. Since Jshap denied interest in her, I presume his presence in the house is keeping her off.

As soon as my new apartment is habitable, when I’m done with furnishing it, I’ll let him know about my movement. And when I’m fully integrated into the group, I’ll come back to take Lizzy, and if he, or Steel or anyone else stands in my way, I’ll snuff ‘em.
We stand face-to-face staring at one another speechlessly for a while. Then I tell him to excuse me.
“So you’ve finally made up your mind in spite of my warnings,” he replies.
“Made up my mind to do what? What the hell are you talking about?” I shoot back.
He chuckles.
“Please let me pass, I’m already late for a date.”
“For the initiation you mean.”
“What initiation?”
“There’s nothing I say to you now that will make you change your mind, but I want to assure you that you will surely regret this decision.”
“Since when did you become my keeper, or don’t I also have the prerogative to my life?”

“I’m sure you’ve not heard this popular saying that many who cannot tell apart right from wrong follow the crowd, some who know what is right, but lack the will to do it lose the crown, but a fool is he who despises wisdom and instruction.”
“Please get out of my way,” I growl flushing with anger.
“There is a way that seems right to a man, but in the end it leads to death. That is the path you have just chosen to thread.”

A long lonely road leads to one of the exit from the school premises. There is no moon or stars in the sky, the night is as dark as it can be and the road is deserted. My heartbeat drums in the silent night as I march on briskly listening attentively for the signal. The beam of light from a building casts a tall shadow of a figure coming afar off behind me.
I hear the sound of double clicking of fingers. I was told to listen for the sign and wait.
“Lie down and face the ground now!” comes a voice from a small bush by the side of the road.
I obey the voice covering my face with my palm folded into a fist. Then I hear footsteps coming from the bush.
“Who told you to come here?” one shouts at me.
“I was invited?” I quaver.
“Invited for what?” another voice inquires.
“A party,” I reply.
“What is your password?”
“Green.”
He kicks me on the head and asks, “Where is the card?”
The card has been in my hand since I turned into the road. I unfurl my left palm and one of them takes it from me.
“Where’s the money you were told to bring?”
They take it from my right palm and pull me up by my trousers waist dragging me into the forest to two others whose faces are concealed with facemasks.
“On the floor,” They order me and push me down. “Give me a hundred press ups on your knuckles, fast!”
The ground is hard and full of pebbles, but, it’s no use complaining. I make my hand into a fist and painfully push up and down, while they count.

“Up, up push up you’re not rising. Thirty-one…I say push up…thirty-two…” he shouts. When I grow weary, I stop pushing and lie down on the ground panting heavily. One of them hits my back with a stick. “Get up asshole!” he barks.
They slap and kick me.
“Get up you lazy idiot,” another orders me.
When I stand up, I notice my hands are torn and smeared with blood.

“Do you see those men over there?” he points at three men standing a short distance away from us. One of them is holding a slender bar, which he hits repeatedly on his right palm. I suspect it’s an iron bar.
“Yes,” I pant.
The other boy with him shouts, “Lie down and face the ground.”
I quickly do that.
“Will you get up,” he shouts slapping me on my back and dragging me up. “It’s not you, dummy.”

His colleague rains abuses at another candidate who has just arrived the post, and tears the light-coloured shirt he’s wearing with the dagger he’s holding. When I stand up he points to the direction again and commands me to advance to where they are, leaping like a frog.
“Now move!” he barks.

The screaming from the new candidate subjected to their brutal kicking and beating fades as I leap away from their post. When I reach the next post, one of the boys clenches his fist and blows me hard in the stomach and I fall down puffing and panting.
“Turn your back, turn your back,” they bark at me.
“And stretch your hands forward,” another commands.

I don’t know how many more posts are remaining but I am shivery from shrieks of agony coming from the posts ahead. None of us will be spared from the brutal thumping and pounding from these cold-blooded cruel beasts.
“Please – o, please, please o – ouch,” I scream as the slender iron rod lands repeatedly on my buttocks and my back.
“Will you shut up! What did you think you were coming here for, a birthday party?”
One of them kicks me hard on my head as I roll over my side and continue begging them. “Will you turn your back!” he barks, “Turn your back you idiot!”
My head spins round and round.
He pushes me and turns me over making me lie face down. “If you touch your ass again I’ll start counting afresh,” the one having the iron rod warns me and the spanking continues.
“I won’t, I won’t touch – aagh, aagh,” I scream out from the painful lashing, “Plea-se, please, please – oh God.”
The other two boys hold my hands and legs to steady my body, while he continues flogging me.
“How many!” The boy with the rod asks me.
“I don’t – I don’t know, I don’t know – please it’s okay,” I whimper.
When he hit me again, I feel a bone crack in my spine. “Ouch!”

“Now look up you lazy weakling,” one of them tells me raising my head forcefully. He points me to another post where three men in black are molesting another victim of their cruelty. “I want you to roll on your sides to the post over there.”

I watch them carry him up swing him side to side three times, and then throw him down a slope from where they’re standing. His yelling gradually fades out as he tumbles down the slope. And they fall about. I can’t believe this hellish game is all for fun. One could breathe his last going through this feat. The thought that there could be well over a dozen more posts ahead makes me tremble.
“Now start rolling on your side to the post over there,” he barks.
“And if you stop on the way you’re a dead man, now move!” another chips in.

Enduring the piercing pebbles and jagged stones on the ground, I toss myself from side-to-side to the next post until I’m wedged at the feet of a giant figure at the post. He picks me up from the ground effortlessly, puts me on his shoulder and turns round and round several times. Then he falls backwards and both of us land on the floor, on my back, with his weight on me.
“Hmm,” I heave.
His colleagues descend on me immediately he gets up kicking and clubbing me cruelly as if they mean to kill me.

After, the giant takes my arms and the other two hold my legs. They swing me sideways three times and throw me backwards down the slope. Landing on my back, I roll down the slope until a large stone hits my ribs and abruptly stops my sideways gallop. For a moment I thought I was going to die. But it seems it’s just the beginning of the fun for this bunch of bullies. Two of them stationed at the foot of the hill hurriedly rush towards me.

I’m black and blue and I’m exhausted. I can’t move any further, my hands are sore and my legs are numbed from the incessant poundings. My eyes are bruised and I’m losing my sight as I peer helplessly at the two bullies running towards me.
“Why did I come here, why did I come here,” I mumble.

When the gruesome drill is over, we are made to lie down facing the ground musing over the ordeal we have been through not knowing what will come next. Most of us are in a critical state health-wise after repeated violent pounding and battering, flogging and clubbing, punching and kicking through thirty-two posts manned by three or at least two masked men.

One of us, who is bare-bodied and has his head broken and bleeding, howls bitterly against his friend who deceived him into coming for the haze – he was told to accompany him to a party. Some others in hushed tone curse themselves for accepting the invitation and curse their friends who invited them.

One of the masked men referred to as the High Priest comes to us and addresses us. He calls the names of six of the candidates lying down with me, including the one with a broken head, and asks them to stand up. Four masked men who came with the High Priest lead them away from us – they have failed the test. Two of them had passed out during the drill and were only resuscitated hours later.

The High Priest congratulates us and tells us that we have done well and that we have qualified for the next stage of the initiation – a test of intelligence, which will take place in three days time. He also warns us sternly that neither angels nor any other mortal must know where we had been or hear about what happened here.

We are sent home before the dawn of the day, at fifteen minutes past four with the charge to think over again if we really want to join the brotherhood, otherwise such a person is free to excuse himself from the second stage.

Before going for the initiation, I had taken the decision to go to my new apartment instead of returning to meet Jshap. He’d demand an explanation for the contusion on my face and the cuts on my body.
THIRTY-SEVEN

We are asked to lie down near a small old and dilapidated house, which they refer to as the shrine, while the men in mask dressed in dark shirts, black trousers and black berets march slowly into the God-forsaken house one after another in a single file bearing a burning candle each and croaking repeatedly, “My mother don’t you worry, my father don’t you worry, if I happen to die an inverted man, never mind we shall meet again…my darling don’t you worry, my teacher don’t you worry, if I happen to die an inverted man never mind we shall meet again.”

Twenty-eight of us made it through to the second stage of the hazing, which is generally referred to as blending. We are reminded that only nuggets, that’s us, that pass the intelligence test, codenamed etit, will move on to the final test after which successful nuggets will be accepted into the brotherhood and thereafter have the honour of becoming one of the campus hard men.

Gideon Dim is lying next to me as we eagerly wait to be called up for the intelligence test. Gideon and I have already started courting a mutual feeling of brotherhood. Apart from the fact he’s my faculty mate, when we met the day after the drill and identified each other as nuggets, after lectures he offered to buy me lunch. After being through the drill, the feeling of toughness and self-confidence was evident in our carriage and smug gait as we walked into the cafeteria wearing dark sunshades.

Gideon quizzed me about my motive for wanting to belong, and asked me if I had a chip on my shoulder about anything. I said no. When he pressed me I told him that it was sheer adventure that stirred my interest. He pledged his support for me should I need his help. I reciprocated, swearing the oath of allegiance of a brother’s keeper to him.

Then he opened up and told me he has a grudge with a certain fellow who collected money from him fraudulently and has threatened to kill him if he ever asks him of it or hints reporting the matter to the school security. He said he’s never felt so belittled. That was what led to his decision to join a cult group, any cult group. He told me in confidence over our meal that as soon as the initiation is over, he’ll pay him a visit and issue him an ultimatum on when he’s coming to collect his money. He amuses himself with his likely reaction at his visit when he realizes that his level has changed.

We are intimated of the rule of the intelligence test – we will be summoned into the small house in pairs and asked a question to test our aptitude, the first person to answer the question correctly qualifies for the next stage of the initiation.

So far only two people have been able to answer the questions correctly. Gideon whispers to me that after all he’s been through and coming this far, he can’t imagine failing this test. He is paired with another nugget called Dapo Popoola. I wish him luck.

“My ping-pong ball falls into a hole just enough to let the ball pass through. Can any one of you suggest to me how I can bring the ball out of the hole in five seconds?” one of the boys who the High Priest introduced as Basket Case asks them.
Both of them remain silent for a moment. Then Gideon answers, “Fill the hole with water.”
“What happens when you fill the hole with water?” he asks Gideon.
“Because of its light weight the ball will float on the surface of the water.”
There is a round of applause for Gideon.
“Compose!” the one moderating the show yells for them to be quiet, “Gideon Dim is taken,” he announces. Then they are ushered out of the room.
“Next, Ugochukwu Amanze!”

I shudder at the mention of my name and rise up and march into the house. They’re all squatting on the ground with their backs against the wall. Although their faces are covered with black facemasks, I am able to recognize Steel. His pair of crutches, which is leaning on the wall behind him, gives him away. He is distinctly dressed in a black trench coat and a skull cap. Besides, he’s the only person sitting down in the only chair in the room.

The moderator, standing in the middle of the room and holding a small sheet of paper in his hand, calls the last candidate for the test as I approach him, “And lastly Ezulike Okoye.” Then he squeezes the sheet of paper and put it his mouth and starts chewing it.
He walks into the room self-assuredly with a smug smile on his face. We stand face-to-face to one another.
“Silent thunder,” the moderator beckons one of the boys in mask.
“Shezou Pagan,” he replies.
“Come forward and ask your question.”

Silent thunder collects his neighbour’s dagger and stands up. He brings out his own dagger from the sheath as he walks up to us. When he comes to where we are standing, he places them in his palms and asks us to take a dagger each. Ezulike is obviously too eager and goes first. I take a quick glance at the guy in the facemask, and then take the dagger from him.

“Supposing you both cut yourselves with these knives to enter into a blood covenant to keep a matter secret, which is known to just two of you, how can you really keep that secret secret?” he asks stepping backwards away from us.
Ezulike’s smile vanishes and he cocks his head pondering over the question.
“I’ll stab him to death,” I reply coldly.
“Yo Shinai!” Silent thunder says.
The boys clap their hands in applause and chorus after him, “Good thinking, good thinking.”
“Ugo Amanze is taken,” the moderator announces to the house.
After, we are all led into the shrine and made to stand in a circle around a symbol on the floor with candles arranged in a pattern around a skull and cross bones in the middle of the house.

He turns to where Steel is sitting and announces, “Sixteen nuggets have qualified for the final stage of the inversion process.” Then he turns to us. “Your final test will be communicated to you in due time. On behalf of the spiritual head of this family, I congratulate you all for having come this far. It is our desire that you all succeed in your final test to become one of us. Until we meet again keep the faith, and don’t forget, keep our secrets secret… you may now leave the shrine in a single file.”
Some of the nuggets that failed the aptitude test like Ezulike were admitted by proxy. I suppose they were considered either based on their performance during the drill, or personal interests.

I was informed that somebody will meet me at the department of English building. I was told precisely that at three-thirty this afternoon I should be seated on the wooden balustrade at the ground floor of the block so that the courier who will come to instruct me about my next assignment can identify me.

My departmental building is nearly three hundred metres from here and in order not to be late I leave early and arrive here at ten minutes past three and sit at the balustrade quietly waiting for the contact. Although we were warned not to let anyone else hear about the instructions we were given, Gideon informed me his rendezvous is at Lanre Oduduwa building, the department of Political science. I suppose our designated rendezvous are chosen in such a way that nobody who is acquainted with any of us will have the slightest hint what transpires between the faceless members of the group and us.
Two boys wearing dark sunglasses saunter towards the balustrade where I’m sitting. One of them is wearing a black beret and the other a baseball cap.
“Did you receive a visa to Benin Republic?” the one wearing a baseball cap asks me.

“Yes, but there’s a capital flight,” I reply. That’s the line I was given. I’m acquainted with secret codes from my days in the palm fronds way back in secondary school, although we never initiated anyone else, it was just me, Ganza, Paul, Timo and Reuben.
“Do you still intend to travel to that country?” he asks.
“If I’m promised a red carpet reception,” I reply.
The one wearing the beret who had been silent and vigilantly looking to the left and right asks me, “What do you know about Benin Republic?”
This is an unscripted question. I’ve never been to that country, but I know they are not interested in hearing any detail about Benin Republic. “Of course it shares the same border with Nigeria.”

Both of them chuckle. “You are obviously not good at current affairs,” he jests. “Well, the point of entry into Benin Republic is the hardest point and if you are caught, you are deported straight away,” he continues, “But if you make it into that country, every other thing becomes easy.”

“And your dreams come true,” the one wearing a baseball cap chips in. He looks over his shoulder to his right and then to his left and brings out a photograph from a file jacket he’s carrying and shows it to me. “Do you know this guy?” he asks.
“No I don’t,” I reply.

“His name is Demola,” he whispers as he put the photograph back in the file. “He was arrested by the police a few days ago. He is presently in detention, and he’s about to be arraigned in three days. He has a reputation of betraying secrets and so we think his testimony will jeopardize the integrity of our family.”

His colleague takes off his beret and brings a small black bottle and gives it to him as he takes a pause. He passes the bottle on to me. “Our statement of allegiance is to rather give up your life than give out our secret,” he hesitates, “By all means you must ensure that Demola does not appear before the Judge. We do all it takes to protect our secrecy. I’m sure you understand what I mean?”
I gaze at the bottle in my hand and shudder at the sign of the skull and crossbones on it – the content is lethal.

“You are to visit Demola tomorrow. Visiting hours is from ten to four p.m. You are free to choose anytime that suits you. We will be pleased to hear that Demola will not be appearing before the Judge…is that clear?”
“Shezou,” I reply.
He looks to his right and left and slaps me on my left cheek. “Don’t ever use that word again! Am I clear?”
“Yeah.”
“Good.” he taps me on my shoulder and steps away.
“Don’t forget they may ask you to taste the food before accepting it from you,” his colleague adds, taps my shoulder and quickly joins him.

I caress my chin and ponder over the task before me. The mystery book better be with these guys, or else…and when I get it. I may just decide to keep it for myself. Then I’ll rule the cult and make all other groups submit to me. And according to Jshap, make things happen, a lot of things. I brood over it as I watch them walk away.
THIRTY-EIGHT

Today is the final stage of the initiation process. The men in masks march into the shrine silently, holding a burning candle each. But unlike the previous meeting, they aren’t singing. We are summoned in after they had all entered.

From his voice I notice that the one referred to as the High Priest is the same person who played the role of an emcee during the intelligence test. Apart from the full black regalia he’s wearing, he has a red band around his right upper arm. Two others are wearing similar armbands. I guess they are the principal officers of the group, but in addition the High Priest is wearing a red sash around his waist.

We are made to stand in a circle bowing our faces to the altar – a portion on the floor marked off in the middle of the room by a six-sided star drawn with a red paint. A skull and two bones are placed inside the star surrounded by candles of different colours. There is also a pot of fire with strange inscriptions written in different colours raised on a metal tripod stand.

“Compose!” the High Priest yells as Steel hobbles into the shrine accompanied by two others – one is holding a long sword, while the other is holding a small black coffin. He is wearing a black trench coat and a black beanie with cross sign inverted, and he’s wearing a black armband on his left arm and a black sash about his waist.
“All hail the Cardinal!” the High Priest shouts.
They all remove their berets holding it to their chest and standing at ease, they chorus, “Shezou Cardinal!”

There is silence in the room as Steel limps into the room with his back facing us. And eventually sits down on the only chair in the room. The two boys come to the altar and drop the sword and the coffin. They turn back and bow to him then join the others who are standing. The black coffin has a bronze cross nailed on its cover, in the reverse direction, upside-down.
“All hail the Cardinal,” the High Priest shouts again as he comes to the altar facing us.
“Shezou Cardinal!” we all respond with the older guys.
“Shezou!” Steel replies, “Let the inversion proceed.”

“On behalf of his eminence the Cardinal and the Hangman in absentia, I the High Priest of this fraternity congratulate and welcome sixteen of you who made it to the final stage of the inversion process,” the High Priest announces and takes a pause, “Since all of you have decided, of your free will, to become members of this family, the process of inversion shall proceed with the swearing of the oath as contained in the sacred book of faith, the irrevocable testament of the Inverted Cross Fraternity, the third chapter and third verse.”
That should be the book, I say to myself.

The High Priest ambles to the smaller coffin, the one that one of the boys that accompanied Steel brought. In defiance of the instruction given to us, I raise my head slightly and peek at the contents of the coffin to see if I can catch a glimpse of the book.
One of the boys standing directly opposite me marches briskly to me and gives me a hot slap at the back of my head. “Face down!” he barks. I recognize his voice. It’s Sammy, Steel’s friend.
I obey him.
He turns and goes back to where he was standing near one of the exits. I can feel his eyes are on me.

The High Priest opens the small coffin and brings out a bunch of paper cut into small sheets and a black box the size of a matchbox. He beckons one of the men in mask to come and assist him distribute the sheets of paper to us.
“You will recite the oath written on the paper mentioning your names,” the High Priest tells us.

“I, Ugochukwu Amanze hereby take this oath to become a disciple of the Inverted Cross Fraternity. I promise to abide by the tenets of the family as contained in the irrevocable testament of the Inverted Cross Fraternity. I also vow that I will never divulge the secrets of this family to anyone. By the shedding of blood I enter and by the shedding of this blood only can I leave.”

“I hereby put this scar on your wrist as a token of your allegiance to the Inverted Cross Fraternity. As long as this remains, you are bound by the oath you have sworn today in the presence of these witnesses. Will you accept the scar according to your confession?” the High Priest responds.
“I will.”
“Raise your left hand.”
I obey him, making my palm into a fist.

His assistant comes with a slender rod with which the others have been marked. The rod is returned back to the fire after each nugget is marked. He looks at the tip to be sure of the position of the cross and punches my wrist removing the rod almost immediately.
“Sshh!” I twitch.

The area of burnt skin immediately turns white revealing a tiny mark of inverted cross on my wrist. Then blood is let out of the wound. The priest gives the rod back to his assistant and dabs the wound with the sheet of paper I was given. The bloodstain leaves the mark of an inverted cross on the paper. He gives the paper back to me with the instruction, “Fold the paper and hold it.”
When every one us has recited the oath and received the mark on our wrists, he collects the folded sheets of paper from all of us, throws them into the pot of fire and takes it to the Cardinal.

“By this oath these sixteen nuggets pass through the fire of inversion to become disciples of the inverted cross and as the fire burns so shall they perish if they ever seek to break this oath. May it please the Cardinal who stands in the place of the supreme Hangman who is absent at this meeting to acknowledge the oath,” he declares briskly. Then he returns the pot to the metal tripod stand in the altar.

Steel, the Cardinal takes a small bottle from the High Priest and empties the content, a black liquid, into the pot. The fire suddenly rises nearly torching the High Priest and the Cardinal. We all scamper fearfully away from the pot. Then Steel returns to his seat while the High Priest comes back to us.

The other disciples continue to chant their peculiar choruses waiting, I guess, for the fire to recede. The High Priest then carries the pot to Steel. He pours a black powder from another small bottle similar to the one that contained the oil.

“This is the salt of the covenant of the spirit of Abadon, the prince of the bottomless pit and god of all disciples of the inverted cross to whom you have pledged allegiance at the cost of your lives, with your blood,” he declares raising his hand. Then he pours the black sand or salt or whatever into the pot. The fire in the pot dies out instantly.
“All hail the Cardinal who stands in the place of the spirit of the Hangman!”
“Shezou Hangman! Shezou Cardinal!” the men in masks respond in chorus with a deep voice.
“Let the nuggets hail the Cardinal who stands in the place of the spirit of the Hangman!”
“Shezou Hangman! Shezou Cardinal!” we all respond after our senior colleagues.

“I hereby present these men as disciples of the inverted cross, having successfully passed through the inversion,” the High Priest continues, “In the absence of the Hangman, the first disciple of the inverted cross, I hereby request the Cardinal to commence the unveiling process.”

We are asked to give way as the Cardinal steps into the centre of the altar into our midst. He removes his hat and slowly pulls his mask off. Then he turns around ponderously so we can all behold his face. “I am Ebubedike Nwizu, final-year mechanical engineering. I am popularly known as Steel, but my inverted name is Gehenna. I am the Cardinal …you are welcome.”
“Shezou Gehenna! Shezou Cardinal!” they all chorus cheerfully.
“Next, the Rainmaker,” the High Priest announces.

One of the boys wearing the red armband steps out and comes to the altar. He takes off his beret and slowly pulls off his mask. His colleagues cheer him and call his nickname. “Braimo Simpson, fourth-year architecture, I am known as Azure, my inverted name is Canister. I am the Rainmaker.”

Another principal officer comes and takes off his beret and unmask. “Caleb Onyedika, third-year law. I am known as Confusion but my inverted name is Migraine. I am the Navigator.” Then he steps back at the echo of his inverted name by his colleagues.

The return of the moderator throws the house into a frenzied stir as he paces into the altar to introduce himself. He takes off his black beret and slowly removes the black veil. “Omololu Adeleke, final-year law. They call me Pagan, but I prefer Black rain.”
“Black rain! Black rain!” his colleagues applaud him.
“I am the High Priest,” he concludes. “Now all sons of darkness will unveil before our new brothers beginning with the satraps,” he announces.

One ambles to the middle area and removes his beret and then his mask – it’s Olumide. He smiles mischievously as he stares at me. “Olumide Ore, third year banking and finance nickname Bricks code name Silent thunder,” he says in clipped tone and goes back to where he was standing.
When our eyes meet I smile lightly to him. Olumide winks at me in return.
After the unveiling process, the first thing we learn about the family is the fraternal handshake. All members of the family greet us excitedly with the fraternal handshake.

A locally brewed wine is passed from one disciple to another amidst the chanting of choruses, drumming and dancing as the house celebrates with us. They call it sensibrew. Some say it is the drink of the gods, while others refer to it as the drink of wise men. It is passed from one disciple to another. In the spirit of brotherhood, no cup was provided so we are all made to drink merrily from the can.

Punch, which is called sensibrew here, is a flavoursome and strong beverage made from a concoction of raw ethanol, stout, black currant, and Indian hemp essence, and sweetened with sugar. The Indian hemp is boiled in water long enough to give off much of its essence. Then it is later sieved and raw ethanol is added to the solution. Black currant drink is added to give it a wine-red colour and stout to enrich its flavour. The concentrated solution is eventually diluted with water and sweetened with sugar.

Everyone is allowed to take a mouthful of this highly intoxicating concoction at one time as it keeps coming around until we are all high. It’s a moment of joy for everyone, especially we, the pupa, who have looked forward to this after the gruesome hazing. We give one another the fraternal handshake grinning from ear to ear. Gideon is over the moon and I think he’s smashed too. He whispers to me that he will report the guy that defrauded him to the family as soon as the party is over.
THIRTY-NINE

My inverted name is Albatross. My first outing to prove the clout of the new fraternity comes on the heels of our initiation. Thanks to Gideon. He took his case to the family and demanded for justice. The principal officers agreed that a fast track solution be adopted to recover the money since the guy in question, who is in his final year, will be leaving school after the exams.

Gideon suggested that he and I be given the permission to visit the guy and demand for the money. But for the fear that we may either handle the matter prematurely or not have the courage to challenge him because he’s a notorious cultist, Sammy, who was also appointed as my guardian angel, was asked to accompany us.

The victim, Obi Shina stays off campus, in the boys’ quarters of a lecturer’s house. Our visit is essentially to put out feelers so as to ascertain the logistics of the next course of action, and to know if an attack will be necessary. For that matter, we aren’t prepared for an assault. None of us has a facemask, and although we were told that the use of firearms would not be necessary, my flute, the fraternity parlance for pistol, is secretly tucked into my bum bag because I’ve gotten used to carrying it wherever I go. And I’m sure there’s a more dangerous arsenal in the rucksack Sammy’s carrying on his back.
Sammy instructs Gideon to go and meet him and talk things over with him one-on-one and see if he’d comply while we wait at a corner watching how it goes.
Gideon was given the inverted name Troubled Waters, but he adopted the name Legion as his nickname saying he’d be so bad his father will regret ever sending him to school.
“You know, I like that nickname, Legion,” Sammy whispers breaking the ice. “What’s your nickname?”
“Hugo Boss.”
“Hugo Boss,” Sammy whispers, “That makes you a power dresser, right?” he says jokingly hitting me slightly on my shoulder.

Everybody has a nickname. In fact people hardly go by their real names. It is widely said that the more wicked, cruel or malicious the name sounds the more appealing it is, the same goes for our inverted names.

Legend has it that a certain parent who came to visit their son inquired of a fellow student about him. The fellow gave the father the impression that he was probably in the wrong place. He told them he’s never heard the name in his life. They left school frustrated. But three weeks later, he was surprised to learn that it was his roommate’s parents that made the inquiry. He never knew him by his real names.
“What does Gehenna mean?”
“Hellfire, you ever heard of that place?”
“Yeah, I’m a Christian.”

“You don’t have to be a Christian to know about hell anyway. If it does exist, that’s where all of us, all disciples of the inverted cross will end up,” Sammy says flatly, “You know…we were actually intending to come and talk you into the family before you and Steel had that face-off in Lizzy’s place.”
“Well, he obviously misunderstood me.”
Sammy laughs at me and replies, “Are you still interested in dating Lizzy?”
“I wasn’t going after her.”
“I said are you still interested in dating her, yes or no?”
“No,” I reply, because I do not know why he’s asking me this question.
“I don’t like that bitch…she knows virtually everything about the family.”

“Steel has told her most of the family secrets. You know, one day she gave me the fraternity’s handshake and asked me if I’d like to travel with her,” Sammy grumbles staring at me. “You don’t know what that means, do you?”
“I don’t, but I don’t see anything wrong with travelling with her?” I answer.

“I wasn’t pissed off because she asked me to, Albatross, but the fact that she communicated it in our parlance. She probably knows more secrets about the family than most of us, and to think of it she’s only a fresher. I have a foreboding she’ll use it against us in the future…I wish he never dated her.” Sammy’s tone of voice is harsh, but I can’t figure out what exactly is going on in his mind. “So how’s it going with you guys, you and Lizzy?”
“I don’t understand.”
“Hey cut out that shit. You’ve been going to her crib,” he says turning to me.
“How do you know that?”
“I’m your guardian angel, have you forgotten?”

Every new disciple is assigned an older disciple to tutor him in the ways of the family. Although we refer to ourselves as sons of darkness, these tutors are called our guardian angels because of the secrecy of our relationship with them – they usually stay afar off and watch the new disciples carry on their lives in accordance with the rules of the family, which had earlier been communicated to us. Once in a while, when the new disciple, now called pupa errs his guardian angel shows up and corrects him. And if there’s any information for the pupa, it’s his guardian angel that passes it across.

This relationship continues for a period of six months, and during this period, they are the only ones we are allowed to relate with outside our usual secret meeting venues. Every pupa receives relevant information from his guardian angel and is free to ask questions relating to the affairs of the family. Within this period a pupa may be excommunicated from the family if he is reported by his guardian angel not to be complying with the rules. If the offence is extreme, elimination of the pupa becomes an inevitable option, we were told.

This strategic relationship is to protect the identity of new members from the security since associating with older members of a cult group gives away the identity of its new members, which is naturally detrimental to the secrecy of the family.
“So that’s how it works?”
“We are a very organized family…does that beat your imagination?”
“Not really,” I answer.
“I didn’t tell Steel about it.”
“I’ll do anything to have them break up,” he says coldly, “But I have a piece of advice for you. Give Lizzy everything she asks for, but your heart, because she’ll break it.”
I chuckle and remain silent.

My association with Sammy hasn’t lasted that long, but I was quick to notice he generally addresses women with insufferable repugnance and he is passionate about the affairs of the family – he goes about it as if it’s his private business.

Sammy is Steel’s best friend. But if he’s urging me to make advances at Lizzy, and he’s also willing to help make the relationship work, I suspect there’s definitely an ulterior motive behind his gesture. I don’t trust him.
“So are they still going out?” I ask him.
“Well, for now Steel has a lot occupying his mind,” he replies.
“I can imagine,” I hesitate, “When are you guys going to retaliate?”
“As soon as we identify the group,” he answers looking at his watch.
“Does it usually take this long to identify a group if you’re attacked?”
“Damn! This guy’s been gone for more than ten minutes. What the hell’s he doing in there?”
“Should I go and look him up?” I ask snappily with excitement.
“Please do, and don’t keep long. Get him out of there and let’s get out’a here.”

Standing at the door for a moment, I eavesdrop into the conversation going on in the room to be sure how many people are in there. I realize it’s only two of them, Gideon and Obi. I can hear Gideon’s voice begging him to spare his life and telling him that he’ll never ask him for the money again if he let him go. Obi threatens to kill him and presses him to tell him what gave him the audacity to come to his room.

I unzip my bum bag, take my pistol and lower the door handle. It is not locked. Gideon is kneeling down in the room. Obi shouts at me as I enter but when he sees the pistol pointing at him he freezes. Gideon stands up and Obi goes on his knees begging us to spare his life.

He asks us to take the money in his shirt pocket hung on a chair by his reading table and promises to pay the interest on the debt tomorrow. Gideon holds the pistol at him while I search the trousers for the money.

I leave off ransacking his wardrobe for extra money when I hear a loud blast behind me and turn around. Gideon pulls the trigger again. I gaze in horror as Obi falls face down with blood dribbling on the blue rug. Gideon raises the hammer for another shot. I rush him and hit his hand off his target. The bullet shatters the window louvers. I take the pistol from him and we scuttle off quickly.

Gideon and I are summoned to appear before the disciplinary panel on a four-count charge of gross misconduct, unlawful use of arms, murder and for jeopardizing the family’s security and integrity. Being at the pupa stage of membership, any of these charges could earn us immediate excommunication from the family if we’re pronounced guilty.

The disciplinary panel has the four principal officers, called the precinct caucus, the Arrowhead, which is Sammy, and the Satraps – eleven district officers – as statutory members. And any three of the four principal officers and seven district officers is the minimum required to make a quorum. Cases such as fighting and disagreements among members, unacceptable behaviour of a disciple in relation to conflict with a member of another cult group, stealing and of course murder are brought before the panel for jurisdiction.

In contrast to the conventional court of law, the Cardinal, after due consultation with the other three principal officers pronounces the accused person as guilty or not guilty, but it is the district officers that recommend the appropriate punishment in the case of a member who is found guilty of the charges brought before the panel.

The punishment for a rape offence is death sentence, if there is a witness and a proof – a foolproof testimony of the victim that the accused person has committed such an offence. An accusation of the battery of a ringo is punishable by severe physical drill, which is aimed at leaving the accused person with a deformity in his member – an eye for an eye, a bruise for a bruise and if any of the victim’s bone is broken, the accused will suffer twice as much.

No member of the family is above the law. Although the Cardinal is the highest-ranking officer and presides over the panel, he may be summoned to appear before the disciplinary panel if the other three officers find him guilty of an offence. But in that case, the district officers do not have the right to proffer punishment although two third of their vote is required to enforce the verdict of the three principal officers, which could either be a temporary suspension from office or abdication of office.

Because nearly all disciples brought to the panel are found guilty, and severe pounding of the accused person usually follows the verdict, it has become convenient for the panel to hold trials in Goshen, where we usually have our meetings, called fellowship of the spirits.

The High Priest reads the charges to the panel after which Gideon and I are asked to move some distance away from them for consultations. Sammy is called up to explain what transpired that night. He knew ahead that he was going to be summoned, because the operation was under his supervision. We all agreed to tell the same story, which I fabricated to save Sammy’s neck.

Sammy is acquitted from the charge of conspiracy of murder, while Gideon and I are found guilty of all charges levelled against us. The Cardinal, as is the usual custom, asked the house if they have any objection to our dismissal from the group, being the appropriate punishment for the offences. To his surprise, Vincent, one of the satraps whose inverted name is Watchtower, a daring stocky fellow, raises his hand in protest.
“Are you objecting the decision, Watchtower?” Steel asks him.

“Yes Cardinal. In fact as far as this honourable bench is concerned, we do not think it is proper to dismiss these young disciples who were caught up in a critical situation that required that they save their lives, from what they have said, which is corroborated by the Arrowhead,” Vincent explains.
“We also heard that the Arrowhead didn’t enter the room with them, he couldn’t corroborate their testimony,” the Cardinal replies.
“That is the more reason they should be given the benefit of the doubt,” Vincent argues further.
“Are you challenging my authority, Vincent?”
“Not at all Cardinal, but –”
“Then sit down,” Steel growls, “How are you sure they are saying the truth in the first place?”

“The arrowhead confirmed their story in his testimony, Cardinal,” Vincent continues, “May I also remind the house that in their testimony, they stated that this plan was sanctioned by the Rainmaker. We all know that sometimes plans don’t go exactly the way they are planned. These young disciples were simply defending themselves. Wouldn’t we have been out there fighting the Tornadoes if it had gone the other way?”

Vincent is an eight over five student of mechanical engineering, same class as Steel. He is the kind of student that is not in a hurry to leave the university, but he doesn’t tolerate the impression that he has no ambition. He’s obviously older than everyone else in the family, but the highest position he’s attained is a Satrap and he doesn’t hide his grievance about it. He has a reputation of picking on the decisions of the precinct caucus.

Twice, I heard, he succeeded in drawing successive Cardinals into an argument, which resulted in their abdicating the post. He had plotted their downfall so that he’d eventually take over as Cardinal, but in both cases another disciple was called to take charge of the family affairs.
“What are you suggesting then Vincent?” Steel asks.
“I suggest that they be given thirty lashes of cane for jeopardizing the family’s integrity as for the charge of murder – they acted in self defence, therefore I urge the panel to strike it off.”
Steel glares at Sammy as he briefly considers the suggestion. “Is this the position of the bench on this matter?”
“Yes,” he replies. His colleagues watch them silently with folded arms.
“What the hell is happening here?” he mutters.

What happened is that when Sammy contacted Vincent about the case, he told him bluntly that the panel will find us guilty, but he said for a price he could get the other eleven district officers to bargain for a lesser punishment and named an outrageous amount to upturn the case. Gideon and I were able to rally fifteen thousand Naira, which he demanded to turn the case around.

“We can as well forget about spanking them in that case,” Steel growls in resignation knowing there has been a conspiracy against him and I’m sure he’s having the foreboding that Vincent could trap him if he pursues the issue further.

Steel refuses to shake hands with Sammy and angrily leaves the forest as soon as the panel dismisses sitting. Sammy, Gideon and I with the other members wade through the shrubs to join the main road leading to school. One after the other at intervals of two minutes we sneak out of the bush to join the road to avoid being spotted by passers-by.
Three of us eventually converge in front of a female hostel where I parked. I unlock the car to let them in.
“Now between three of us, what actually happened in the room that night?” Sammy inquires.
“We told you already Lethal weapon,” Legion begins, “He attacked me with a –”

“Oh please don’t give me that crap, what do you take me for?” he snaps, “My contact in the security department told me they didn’t find any weapon, not even a pen knife on him when they visited his room…why did you have to shoot him?” he quizzes.
Gideon and I glance at one another keeping quiet.
“Who among the two of you shot him?” he presses.
“I did,” I reply.
“Let’s go have a beer Sammy I’ll tell you the truth,” Legion says.

“I don’t go to the beer parlour. Besides, I know the truth. Drop me at Theresa hall. I’m late for an appointment with my cousin,” Sammy replies. “You guys would have put me in real trouble if Steel were a little smarter.”
“Thanks Sammy. You’re really an angel.” I glance at him from the rear-view mirror.
“Thanks bro,” Legion adds.
“You guys better be careful next time.”
“We will,” Gideon and I chorus.

“Can I get some wad off you?” he asks generally, “Like one kay, I’m kind of broke.” After paying Vincent’s fee, Sammy has been milking us, always asking us for money whenever we’re together. He’s pretentiously using the issue as a means to blackmail us and it’s beginning to get on my nerves.
“Well Sammy all I can afford now is three gee,” Gideon tells him.
“How about you Hugo Boss?”
“I don’t have a farthing here on me,” I reply with irritation.
“When can you make it available?”
“You have to give me some time,” I reply almost losing my temper.
“Like when?”
“I don’t know Sammy just give me some time okay.”

I drop him off a few metres to the hall. He says he’d walk the remaining distance for security reasons. I’d do the same if I were in his shoes. Although there is a constant feeling of insecurity about a member of the cult, it is worse when you’re marked as a hit man. Being the Arrowhead, the family’s hit man, he’s the most identified among other cult groups, and he’s branded the most wanted member of the family. Sammy has whacked too many people so he always has wits about him.
“I think he likes you Hugo Boss,” Gideon comments.
I do not respond.
“I’m serious, I doubt if he’d stick out his neck for me if I owned up to snuffing that guy.”
“So what was it you actually wanted to tell Sammy?”
“I think he deserves to know what actually happened. He put himself on the line for us.”
“You know what…no one will ever trust you in this family again if you had done that.”
Gideon takes a quick glance at me and turns away.
“If you are labelled a coward, it means you are liable to divulge the family’s secrets, do you understand that?”
He remains silent.
“Do you know what will happen to you if for any reason you get into trouble and you are nabbed by the police?”
I glance at him and smile shortly.
“They’ll send someone to poison you because they know you’ll spill the beans under duress.”
“But he knows we lied to him. Don’t you think he knows I actually shot Obi? What do you think he meant when he said he knew the truth already?”
“It’s your word against his Legion. Better to appear guilty than gutless.”
“Now I understand. I’m sorry,” he whispers.
“Don’t be sorry okay. You don’t have to be sorry about anything. If you make a mistake, or hurt somebody you shouldn’t be sorry. Just let it be okay. Don’t be sorry.”
“No sentiments right?” he says with a short laughter to show that he feels me.
“Now that he’s dead, what else is your motivation for the fraternity Legion?”
“Well,” he drawls, “I don’t know.”

“You know what, the general impression people have about the cult is that we eat, drink and when we’re smashed we make merry, make love or make trouble, and spend the rest of our days looking over our shoulders in anxiety lest we’re stabbed from behind. I think you have that impression too, Legion.” I hesitate, “But there’s a lot of business going on in the fraternity.”
“Like?”
“Like running the affairs of the students’ union government. Why do you think there’s so much rivalry among various cult groups to control key positions in the government?”
Gideon stares at me with keen interest. “Are you planning to enter the race?”
“No…there’s something big Sammy and Steel are hatching,” I say in a hushed tone.
“How did you get to know about that?”

“I’m not new in this game, Legion.” I glance at him. “This may be a new ground for me, but the rudiments of gangland are always the same…there’s a big runs in the offing. I’ll figure it out with time.”
“You’re only a pupa, Hugo Boss, you think Steel is going to let you in on the deal?”
“I know how to handle Steel.”

When we arrive the hostel, Gideon opens the door but restrains himself, “You know I’ve been troubled in my heart over the death of Obi. I don’t know what came over me that instant. I was so angry I – I lost my senses…do you think I did the right thing?”
“Don’t forget Legion, don’t feel sorry, okay. Those who waste time thinking about the behind don’t do much about the future.”
“I’ll remember that. And if the deal is certain don’t forget me. Who doesn’t want to be rich anyway?” he asks jokingly. “I owe you man…shezou brother.”
“What are brothers for? Shezou.” He gives me the brotherhood handshake and steps out of the car.
FORTY

The slightest offence is brought to the disciplinary panel. And blowing the cover of a pupa is a grave offence. Sammy has broken the rules of engagement as my guardian angel – he’s let everyone know I’m a disciple of the Inverted Cross Fraternity. He borrows my car and dons my baffs whenever he’s going for a meeting with his clients.

But for obvious reasons the panel winks at it. Sammy tells me my personality as a disciple of the Inverted Cross is having a great impact on other frats. Besides, it’s the first time a pupa is so conspicuously identified with the family by nearly everybody few weeks after initiation.

I’ve personally noticed that most of my acquaintances are either keeping me at arms length or being unnecessarily chary in relating with me. I can imagine them whisper silently among themselves about me, “that guy belongs to the most powerful cult group on campus...” and somehow that makes me feel good. But none of them has mustered the courage to ask me about it directly. And I know they won’t.
“What brings you to my class today?” I ask him. Sammy has been leaning on the balustrade waiting for my lecture to end.
He turns around on hearing my voice. “Hi Hugo Boss.”
“Hey, I need your help Hugo Boss,” he says hurriedly.
“What’s it this time?” I ask as we slowly walk away from the stream of students leaving the lecture room.
“What’s your timetable like?”
I know it’s the car he wants. “Well, I’ll be having another lecture in uh –” I look at my watch, “say two hours time.”
“Can you spare me the car for a while, please?” He sounds desperate.
“I’m sorry Sammy, it won’t be possible,” I sigh, “Actually I had fixed a date within this free period. How about using it during the lecture period, is that okay?”
“Please Hugo Boss, I need it now,” he begs.

I knew we’d come to this point and I’ve been laying this trap for him. I won’t let this opportunity slip away. I’ll make him talk, and I’ll get him to consent to giving me a slice of this deal. “I can’t give it out now Sammy,” I insist, “You may have to come back for it later.”
Sammy’s face falls and he remains speechless.
“So what’s about this runs you’re tidying with Steel and Vincent, don’t you want me to know about it? I may be of help.”
“Okay, okay, if that’s what you want we’ll talk about it as soon as I’m done with this meeting okay.”
“You’re posting me.”
“Of course not!
“We agreed to keep it secret because of the person involved. But how the hell did you get to know that Steel and Vince are involved in it?”
“I’ve been counting your steps Lethal weapon…so, are you going to share this secret with me for all my services or not?”
“Tomorrow, I promise, but please let me use the car. It’s almost coming through. I’ve got to tidy up a few things with our link man.”
I throw the car key to him. He grabs it in the air and leaves in a haste.

Two fast knocks, one, and three slow knocks is rattled on the door. It’s only a few minutes past five in the morning. I wonder who could be knocking at my door by this time. I sluggishly rise from the bed. My head is pounding. I’m having a hangover from yesterday night’s indulgence.

“There’s a big problem Hugo Boss,” he says as he barges into my room. “The police have waded into the murder of Obi. Somebody saw us and went to inform the police about what happened, they mentioned my name and gave them a description of you and Legion.”

The ache in my head suddenly disappears. I gape at Sammy speechlessly not knowing what to say. Sammy quietens and lies on the bed with his hands clasped behind his head. “I’ll be blanketing for a while until the pressure dies down,” he mumbles. “Steel is furious. He’s mad with you and the precinct caucus has reached the decision to excommunicate you.”
“They can’t do that!” I blast, “It was Legion who shot him, Sammy.”

“I knew it all along. But what difference does it make now, they’re acting on your word. Besides, the pistol belongs to you, doesn’t it? You think Legion is such a fool to own up to pulling the trigger. Don’t be deceived my friend, nobody wants to die, not for someone else’s offence.”
“How about you, Sammy?”

“Me?” he replies, “Mine is a piece of cake. It happens every now and then, they’ll return to their base after a while. In the worst-case scenario I may have to skip some of my courses during the exams,” he sits up on the bed, “It’s better to have a case with the police than to break the slightest rule in the family. If Steel insists on not allowing you to be arrested, you know what that means?”
I do. Is this the retribution of poisoning Demola to death?
“I’ll advise you as a friend to forget about your studies, go somewhere where they can’t find you,” he says in a hushed tone.
I could actually do that – bolt out of school, but not yet. And what about the book…what about Lizzy? No I won’t. I won’t give it to Steel. I’ve got to find a way to stop him.

Even though I’ve not personally informed Paul I’ve got my foot in the door into the Inverted Cross Fraternity, I’m sure he’s already aware. And I’m sure he’s heard the news of Obi’s death and that the family is on the heat at the moment. Barrister Ilaje, in particular, will be happy to hear that I’m putting my best foot forward in actualising the plan.

If Paul is right about his wide connection in government circles, I’ll talk Paul into persuading him to make the police issue a communiqué that the real culprit has been apprehended and is in their custody. With that Sammy will be let off the hook and I know Sammy, he’ll force the panel to reverse their decision to have me excommunicated.
Luckily, Paul’s car is at the car park and music is playing in the house. He has a female visitor. After our exchange of pleasantries, he asks her to excuse us. She goes into his room.
“I thought you rejected the offer after our first meeting when you didn’t get back to me again,” he says.
“I had to take time to discover things for myself before getting back to you. The group is a dangerous group Paul, I could be killed if they discover I’m spying on them.”
“I know the strength of the inverted cross,” he says smugly and goes to the bar to fix a drink for both of us. “It’s good to know you’re now one of them. How was the initiation?”
“Man, it was brutal. At some point I thought I was going to die.”
“You think you’re called a hard man for nothing.”
“I’ve not sighted the book though, but I know it exists. A section of it was mentioned during our initiation.”

“Traditionally, it is only used when a new Cardinal is ordained by the Hangman. But that changed since the last Hangman disappeared. That’s when the influence of the group began to dwindle, and it became necessary to proscribe it and start all over again.” he replies as he walks back to the chair.
“What do you mean start all over again?” I ask him as I take a glass of Gin and lime from him.

“That’s really who we are. The reformed Inverted Cross Fraternity. You see, the Inverted Cross Fraternity and us come from one stock, a higher fraternity called the Grand Order of Dukes. That’s where the fraternity was conceived. But after the disappearance of the former Hangman, who had a place as the star bearer with the GODs –”
“That’s the Order, right?” I cut in.

“Exactly. The Provost hijacked the fraternity afterwards and began to use it for his own selfish ends. He has a lot of loyalists in the fraternity and Steel is one of them. The order was irked when he began to keep them in the dark about the activities of the fraternity. Barrister Ilaje challenged him and agitated for his removal as the superintendent of the fraternity on the grounds that he was responsible for the fraternity’s dwindling influence because of the missing book.”
“So, has there not been a Hangman after him?”
“Well, no other disciple could occupy the position until the Hangman fully sheds his fingers,” he replies.
“What does that mean?”

“At his ordination, the Hangman is given five bronze nails, called the fingers of the Hangman, which he must use up in his crucifixion parade before handing over the mantle. But he illegally passed on the mantle to his friend, who was the Cardinal, who eventually abdicated the post and disappeared. According to Barrister Ilaje, the Provost retrieved the insignia of office from the Cardinal, but didn’t return the book to the Order,” Paul takes a pause and sips from his glass. “The post will remain vacant until the former Hangman is confirmed dead.”
“What if the Hangman actually handed over the book to the Cardinal?”

“Everyone believed that the Provost kept back the book for personal use,” he continues, “But Barrister Ilaje became suspicious about the disappearance of both the book and its custodian so he consulted the Oracle, the spiritual mediator of the covenant who revealed to him that the book is here.”
“With the former Hangman?”
“Yes. But what the Oracle didn’t say is where he is. No one knows his whereabouts, except the Provost,” he replies, “The Hangman is his nephew.”
“If your group, whatever it’s called –”
“Point of correction, our group,” he says with a smile.

“Okay, our group,” I say with emphasis, “is of the same stock with the Inverted Cross Fraternity, as you said, that means a member of – our group can also be recognized by the Order as a Hangman and custodian of the book.”
“That’s right, that’s our plan.”
“Tell me the truth, Paul. What did he offer you?”

“Well, Hugo Boss. I was hoping you’d ask…and I’ll give you an honest answer. When Barrister Ilaje first mentioned about the book, I had no interest in taking such a risk. So I didn’t have any grounds to bargain with him.”
“So you are doing this for free, you of all people?” I chuckle, “You expect me to believe you?”

“The GODs is a prestigious group, quite distinct among other cults, Hugo Boss. I’ve always had the dream of being a Duke, and Barrister Ilaje promised me he’ll make that dream come true if I help him get the book.”
“I thought students are not admitted into the Order?”

“Yes of course, only Cardinals and disciples who distinguished themselves in their service in the fraternity while they were students. Except the Hangman, who is a permanent member of the Order.” Paul and I stare at one another as he breaks to let me consider his words. “But the Provost banished our group from ascending to become Dukes.”
“Apart from the money Barrister Ilaje is offering me, what else do I gain?”
“It’s up to you Hugo Boss. What else do you want?”
I think I’ll keep the book to myself if I find it, I reason.
“Do you remember what I told you about the hit at Lizzy’s birthday party, that it was a message?”
“Yeah, and may I ask, who actually was the message meant for?”

“Him, the Hangman. The blind target hit was his brainchild, but since Iyke disappeared, no one has ever attempted it. He had this mysterious aura about him that the lights go off at his will when he steps into a place,” he replies.
“Is that his name?”
“Yeah, Ikenna. His inverted name is the Seagull. It was the book that enabled him to do the blind target.”
“I will find the book,” I pause and take a deep breath, “if it’s there.”
Paul squints at me. The smile on his face betrays his excitement.
“But first I need your help. A friend of mine is being hunted by the police over the recent killing of a student.”
“Obi? I heard about it.”

Sammy is also well known to Paul and he’s conversant with the fraternity’s philosophy of guardian angel, since according to him they (or we) come from the same stock. So I go straight to narrating what happened in Obi’s room that night and how the family has reached the decision to excommunicate me, and that someone reported seeing Sammy at that time. Now he’s hunted by the police for interrogation.
